**Summary.** The paper develops a theoretical analysis showing that cavity optomechanical systems can act as highly sensitive quantum sensors. By tuning the system parameters to induce an effective dynamical singularity, the estimation precision (quantified by QFI) diverges as the perturbation strength decreases. This demonstrates a controllable mechanism for achieving enhanced metrology using standard measurement techniques.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a concrete theoretical framework demonstrating how engineered nonlinear coupling (optomechanics) can be used to actively control system dynamics toward singular points that enhance fundamental measurement limits beyond standard scaling laws.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The research aims to harness strongly amplified dynamical responses in cavity optomechanical systems for achieving enhanced quantum sensing and metrology.

**Main result.** By tuning the system near a dynamically induced singularity, the Quantum Fisher Information (QFI) exhibits divergent scaling with decreasing perturbation strength, leading to significantly suppressed estimation error.

**Method.** The analysis employs Gaussian estimation theory, utilizing input-output formalism in the frequency domain and solving for Green's functions to quantify the sensitivity via QFI and CFI.

**Model / system.** The system is a cavity optomechanical setup where an optical mode couples to a mechanical oscillator via radiation pressure. The dynamics are modeled using linearized quantum Langevin equations derived from the full Hamiltonian in the rotating frame.

**Key observables.** Quantum Fisher Information (QFI), Classical Fisher Information (CFI), and the output field covariance matrix ($\mathbf{V}_{	ext{out}}^F[\omega]$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The system is analyzed by tuning parameters to achieve a singular regime where $\det \mathcal{H}_{	ext{eff}} = 0$, which maximizes sensitivity.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Linearization of dynamics around mean fields, use of Gaussian estimation theory, and assuming the probe field noise can be treated via input-output formalism.

**Figures summary.** Figures schematically illustrate the setup (optics coupled to mechanics) and show comparisons between scaling behaviors ($\delta^*	heta$ vs. $	heta$) in both singular (enhanced sensitivity) and non-singular regimes (saturation).

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from defining the physical system and deriving linearized equations of motion, moving into the frequency domain analysis using scattering matrices to determine output field statistics, culminating in the application of quantum estimation theory to identify optimal sensing regimes.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We show that strongly amplified dynamical responses in cavity optomechanical systems can be harnessed for enhanced quantum sensing. By tuning the optomechanical interaction to a regime of enhanced susceptibility, weak perturbations produce disproportionately large changes in the system response, leading to substantially improved estimation precision. Using Gaussian estimation theory, we demonstrate that the quantum Fisher information exhibits a divergent scaling as the perturbation strength decreases, implying a corresponding suppression of the estimation error. We further show that heterodyne detection of the output cavity field yields the classical Fisher information with the same asymptotic scaling as the quantum Fisher information, demonstrating that the enhanced sensitivity is accessible with a standard measurement protocol. These results identify amplified optomechanical dynamics as a controllable resource for quantum enhanced sensing and metrology.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper analyzes the complex collective dynamics, including various synchronization modes, within a 1D anisotropic Heisenberg ferromagnetic spin chain. By solving the LLGS equation, the authors demonstrate that while large chains tend toward desynchronization, applying a field-like torque can successfully restore coherent inphase or complete synchronization. The findings confirm the simultaneous existence of multiple oscillatory states depending on external driving fields.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work explores collective dynamics and emergent order (synchronization) in a quantum spin chain using classical/semi-classical equations of motion. The restoration of coherence via specific field terms is relevant to understanding dissipation and long-range correlations in many-body systems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study investigates different oscillatory modes, including synchronization states (complete, inphase, antiphase), within a one-dimensional anisotropic Heisenberg ferromagnetic spin chain.

**Main result.** The field-like torque is shown to be capable of restoring synchronization that is otherwise lost as the number of spins increases. The system exhibits multiple coexisting dynamic modes depending on applied fields.

**Method.** The dynamics are analyzed by solving the Landau-Lifshitz-Gilbert-Slonczewski equation numerically and comparing results with analytical derivations for oscillation frequencies.

**Model / system.** The model is a one-dimensional anisotropic Heisenberg ferromagnetic spin chain, whose evolution is governed by the LLGS equation. The Hamiltonian includes terms related to nearest-neighbor interactions ($A, B, C$) and external fields.

**Key observables.** Oscillatory modes (complete synchronization, inphase/antiphase synchronization, desynchronization), standard deviation ($\sigma$), and oscillation frequency ($f$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The system is studied by varying the number of spins ($N$) and applying specific field-like torques ($eta$, $H_x, H_y, H_z$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on solving the LLGS equation and making approximations to derive analytical expressions for synchronized oscillation frequencies.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate time evolution of spin components ($S_x$), the loss/restoration of synchronization quantified by $\sigma$, and phase difference plots confirming various synchronization states (inphase, antiphase).

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from defining the physical model and governing equation to numerically demonstrating the existence and dependence of different oscillatory modes on system size and external torques, culminating in analytical validation.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate the different oscillatory modes, namely, complete synchronization, inphase synchronization, antiphase synchronization and desynchronization in a one-dimensional anisotropic Heisenberg ferromagnetic spin chain consisting of a large number of spins. By solving the associated Landau-Lifshitz-Gilbert-Slonczewski equation for the spins we show the simultaneous existence of the above mentioned oscillatory modes in the spins. We observe that when the number of the spins is large the synchronization is lost between the spins; however, we identify that the field-like torque is able to induce synchronous oscillations of the spins in the chain again. We also confirm the agreement of the numerically obtained values of the frequency of the inphase synchronized oscillations with the analytically obtained values.

</details>

**Summary.** This work generalizes the Mpemba effect concept by defining 'resource-Markovianity' for quantum resource theories. It shows that the operational detection of this effect occurs when a relaxation process breaks the expected monotonic decrease of a resource measure with time. This provides a unified, measure-independent diagnostic tool applicable to both quantum distinguishability and classical thermodynamic ordering.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a powerful, general mathematical tool (resource-Markovianity) to detect anomalous relaxation phenomena (Mpemba effect) in open quantum systems, linking it directly to non-Markovian dynamics.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** To provide a measure-independent operational characterization of the resource Mpemba effect by linking it to the breakdown of 'resource-Markovianity' in free dynamics.

**Main result.** The operational formulation, based on breaking resource-Markovianity, provides a general and measure-independent way to characterize the Mpemba effect across various quantum resource theories.

**Method.** The authors introduce 'resource-Markovianity' as a concept and define the Mpemba effect operationally as the failure of this property during relaxation dynamics.

**Model / system.** The framework is applied to general quantum resource theories, modeling dynamics using Lindblad equations for distinguishability (quantum case) and master equations for probability distributions (thermomajorization/classical case).

**Key observables.** Resource measure $M(ho)$, trace distance $D(ho_1, ho_2)$, and the intermediate matrix norm $L_1(\lambda, t)$.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Time ($t$), resource parameters ($\lambda$), and initial state differences.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes the existence of inverses for both initialization dynamics ($E_\lambda$) and relaxation operations ($N_t$).

**Figures summary.** Figures schematically compare three perspectives (state-based, Pontus-Mpemba, operation Mpemba) showing how resource measures change; specific figures show crossovers in trace distance or $L_1$ norms.

**Paper structure.** The paper develops the concept of resource-Markovianity, establishes the operational definition of the Mpemba effect as its breakdown, and then applies this framework to concrete examples like quantum distinguishability and classical thermomajorization.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The Mpemba effect refers to faster relaxation of states that are initially farther from equilibrium, yet its characterization is often tied to a chosen distance or resource measure. We introduce resource-Markovianity, an extended concept of quantum Markovianity to quantum resource theories, and formulate the resource Mpemba effect operationally as the breaking of resource-Markovianity by a relaxation operation. This yields a measure-independent operational characterization of resource Mpemba effects in general resource theories, together with quantitative characterizations based on resource-non-Markovianity measures. We illustrate the framework with the Mpemba effect for distinguishability of states, due to its relation to quantum Markovianity, and with the thermomajorization Mpemba effect from an operational perspective. These results reveal a deep interplay between quantum resources, non-Markovianity, and the Mpemba effect.

</details>

**Summary.** This work introduces Scanning Conductivity and Chemical Potential Microscopy (SCCM), a novel technique that simultaneously maps local conductivity and chemical potential in graphene. By applying SCCM to multilayer graphene, the researchers mapped out complex Quantum Hall Isospin Ferromagnetic phases. The results validate the technique's power by linking single-particle Landau level physics directly to emergent correlated many-body states.

**Why it may be interesting.** The combination of high-resolution nanoscale mapping ($\mu$ vs $\sigma$) with the study of correlated topological phases (QHIFM) provides a powerful experimental platform to test complex many-body theories, which is highly relevant for understanding emergent quantum phenomena in condensed matter systems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The primary challenge was developing a nanoscale probe capable of simultaneously mapping local conductivity and chemical potential within exotic Quantum Hall Isospin Ferromagnet (QHIFM) phases.

**Main result.** The authors successfully demonstrated the Scanning Conductivity and Chemical Potential Microscopy (SCCM) technique, confirming robust QHIFM states in multilayer graphene and establishing quantitative links between single-particle Landau levels and complex correlated ground states.

**Method.** The study combines experimental measurements using SCCM (integrating sMIM for conductivity and KPFM for chemical potential) with detailed theoretical modeling of various stacking configurations (ABAB vs. ABCA).

**Model / system.** The system is multilayer graphene, specifically bilayer and tetralayer structures exhibiting QHIFM physics due to strong electron-electron interactions coupling spin, valley, orbital, and layer degrees of freedom.

**Key observables.** Local conductivity ($\sigma$), chemical potential ($\mu$), Quantum Hall (QH) states, many-body Landau level energy spectrum, and the thermodynamic Density of States (DOS).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Filling factors ($
u = +4$ to $-16$), magnetic field ($B=9$ T), low temperature ($T=100$ mK), and derived parameters like energy gaps ($\Delta$) and critical temperatures ($T_C$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The technique assumes non-interfering measurement of $\sigma$ and $\mu$, and theoretical analyses often treat the system by decomposing it into light/heavy mass sectors, neglecting interaction effects in some models.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate schematic setups for SCCM, maps showing conductivity and chemical potential across QH states in bilayer and tetralayer graphene, and energy spectra detailing Landau level crossings that correlate with phase transitions.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the SCCM technique by demonstrating it on simpler systems (bilayer graphene) before applying it to complex quantum Hall isospin ferromagnetism in marginally twisted double bilayer graphene. It then details how LL crossings modulate the QHIFM sequence and compares results across different stacking orders theoretically.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Quantum Hall isospin ferromagnetism in multilayer graphene offers a versatile playground for exploring flat band correlated physics, driven by the intricate coupling of spin, valley, orbital, and layer degrees of freedom. However, a nanoscale probe capable of simultaneously mapping local conductivity and chemical potential in these exotic phases has yet to be realized. Here, we introduce scanning conductivity and chemical potential microscopy (SCCM), a technique integrating scanning microwave impedance microscopy and Kelvin probe force microscopy. We demonstrate SCCM by probing the quantum Hall states and many-body Landau level energy spectrum in bilayer graphene. Applied to marginally twisted double bilayer graphene, SCCM then reveals a cascade of quantum Hall isospin ferromagnetic states with unexpected re-emergence behaviors. Significantly, experimental many-body Landau level energy spectrum further uncovers the intricate connections of these complex phenomena to inter-subband Landau level crossings and Landau level single-particle wavefunctions. These insights enable the construction of a comprehensive quantum Hall phase diagram. Our results demonstrate SCCM's capability in decoding complex quantum phenomena, establishing it as a versatile nanoscale probe for electron correlation and topology.

</details>

**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.08732v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `methods for driven-dissipative` **3/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **3/5** · `non-equilibrium universality` **3/5** · `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **2/5** · `driven-dissipative phase transition` **2/5** · `Frenkel-Kontorova` **1/5** · `analog quantum simulation` **1/5** · `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **1/5** · `quantum measurements` **1/5**

The corresponding mean surface profiles are displayed in the Fig. S1 in the Supplemental Material.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2606.08732_figures/2606.08732_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 3. Asymptotic scaling laws across diffusivity regimes. Mean wave resistance R(v, ∆) versus diffusivity ∆for five drift speeds v ∈[0.6, 3.0] (a = 0.20, log-log axes). Solid curves: exact evaluation of Eq. (3). At high diffusivities, all curves align sharing the universal ∼∆−5/3 asymptotics of Eq. (5) (Regime I). At low diffusivities, and at subcriti- cal velocities, one obtains the ∼∆asymptotics of Eq. (6) (Regime II), while at critical speed v = 1, one obtains the ∼∆−1/2 asymptotics of Eq. (7) evaluated at δ = 0. At low diffusivities, and supercritical velocities, one approaches the Havelock limit which is insensitive to ∆.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper calculates the mean wave resistance generated by a source moving stochastically at a fluid interface. By incorporating diffusion into classical wave drag theory, the authors derive universal scaling laws that regularize singularities predicted in deterministic models. The results show how noise fundamentally alters the energy dissipation characteristics of the system.

**Why it may be interesting.** While focused on fluid dynamics, the mathematical techniques—especially the use of ensemble averaging over stochastic processes (Brownian motion) to regularize singular deterministic responses—are highly relevant to open quantum systems where noise or environmental coupling smooths out sharp theoretical predictions.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The research aims to determine the mean wave resistance (drag) experienced by a source moving stochastically at a fluid interface, extending classical deterministic theories.

**Main result.** The mean wave resistance exhibits distinct scaling laws depending on the diffusivity ($\Delta$) and drift speed ($v$), notably showing universal high-diffusivity decay $R \sim \Delta^{-5/3}$ and regularization of singularities near critical velocities.

**Method.** The analysis employs Fourier transform methods, ensemble averaging over stochastic trajectories (Brownian motion, Lévy flights), and extensive asymptotic analysis to derive scaling laws in different parameter regimes.

**Model / system.** The system models capillary-gravity waves radiating from a localized pressure source moving along an air–water interface. The dynamics are governed by linearized free-surface boundary conditions for incompressible, inviscid flow.

**Key observables.** Mean wave resistance $\langle R angle$, mean surface profile $\langle\eta(X)angle$.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Dimensionless diffusivity ($\Delta$), drift speed ($v$), critical minimum phase velocity ($c_{\min}$), and the deviation from threshold ($\delta = v-1$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Assumes incompressible, inviscid flow and small-amplitude deformations. The analysis is performed in one dimension by reducing the 2D framework.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate mean-profile asymmetry comparing deterministic vs. diffusive cases; plots show $R(v, \Delta)$ across various diffusivities, demonstrating crossovers between different scaling regimes.

**Paper structure.** The paper develops from a general integral formulation for wave resistance to systematically derive asymptotic forms by applying limiting approximations corresponding to high diffusivity ($\Delta \gg 1$), near-threshold behavior ($v \approx c_{\min}$), and low velocity limits.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Wave resistance is the drag generated by the wave radiation that a source moving at a fluid interface sustains. Under stochastic trajectories, the mean drag is controlled by the ensemble-averaged surface profile built from the trajectory history. We show that the result is a finite resistance below the deterministic radiation threshold and a regularization of the singular response at the minimum phase velocity of the capillary-gravity waves. We derive explicit scaling laws for drifted Brownian trajectories, including a universal high-diffusivity decay. For drifted Lévy flight, we find the mean wave resistance in closed-form, extending wave-drag theory to non-Gaussian trajectories.

</details>

### [Statistical Mechanics of Random Matrices](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08706v1)

**Authors:** Isaac Pérez Castillo  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.08706v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **3/5** · `driven-dissipative phase transition` **2/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5** · `non-equilibrium universality` **2/5** · `Kondo & dissipative impurity` **1/5** · `analog quantum simulation` **1/5** · `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **1/5** · `correlated cavity matter` **1/5** · `entanglement & information structure` **1/5** · `exotic spin models in light-matter` **1/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **1/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2606.08706_figures/2606.08706_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 1: Schematic support conventions for diluted matrix ensembles. In the bi- partite case, the one-mode projection shows how factor nodes generate induced variable–variable couplings.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2606.08706_figures/2606.08706_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 2: Belief propagation and population dynamics as two implementations of the same cavity recursion. On a fixed locally tree-like instance, directed-edge messages are updated by removing the recipient from the incoming neighbourhood. At the ensemble level, the message law is represented by an empirical population, and the same local recursion is sampled as a Monte Carlo fixed-point equation.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2606.08706_figures/2606.08706_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 3: Kesten–McKay versus the semicircle law in the dense-connectivity scaling. The finite-c curves are the analytic random-regular densities after rescaling eigenval- ues by c−1/2; the dotted curve is the Wigner semicircle law obtained when the cavity message distribution concentrates.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2606.08706_figures/2606.08706_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 4: Bipartite cavity structure of a diluted Wishart factor. A sample node µ con- tributes the rank-one matrix d−1x µ(x µ)T to the covariance matrix, producing a clique in the one-mode projection but a single local factor in the bipartite representation. The factor-to-variable message Uµ→i is defined without the overall d−1 prefactor; the contribution entering a variable Green-function denominator is d−1Uµ→i.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2606.08706_figures/2606.08706_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 5: Hermitization as vertex doubling and block-valued cavity messaging. The non-Hermitian problem is lifted from one scalar field per vertex to two fields (ui, vi) per vertex. The block Z i is the bare on-site precision of this doubled local pair, while Ai j gives the cross-layer coupling between doubled neighboring vertices. Conse- quently the cavity Green function sent from i to j is a 2 × 2 matrix; its lower-left entry is the component averaged to form the regularized non-Hermitian resolvent field.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This work presents a rigorous theoretical framework using advanced statistical mechanics to analyze the spectra of sparse and diluted random matrices. It systematically develops methods like the cavity and replica techniques to calculate spectral density and fluctuations. This is crucial for understanding complex systems where local graph structure dictates global spectral behavior.

**Why it may be interesting.** The techniques developed—especially the use of local recursion (cavity) combined with powerful statistical physics tools (replica)—are highly transferable to analyzing complex quantum systems where interactions are sparse or structured, such as disordered spin glasses or lattice models on random graphs.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The primary goal is to develop a statistical-mechanics route to compute spectral properties for sparse and diluted random matrices, moving beyond the standard dense matrix theory.

**Main result.** The paper establishes the mathematical equivalence between the cavity method and the replica method when calculating spectral observables like the density or large deviation functions for these sparse ensembles.

**Method.** It employs advanced techniques from statistical mechanics, including the replica trick, the cavity method (and its associated population dynamics), and saddle-point analysis on functional integrals.

**Model / system.** The focus is on random matrices derived from graphs: sparse symmetric matrices, diluted Wishart ensembles, and non-Hermitian directed/asymmetric matrices. The underlying structure is governed by graph connectivity (degrees, motifs).

**Key observables.** Empirical spectral density ($ho_N(\lambda)$), resolvent functions, eigenvalue counting statistics (large deviations), and conditioned spectra.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Sparsity parameter ($c=O(1)$ in the thermodynamic limit), graph connectivity structure (degrees $k_i$), and the replica index $n 	o 0$.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis often relies on the replica-symmetric ansatz, and the notes are deliberately expanded lecture material rather than a comprehensive review.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 schematically illustrates support conventions for different types of diluted matrix ensembles (undirected, directed, bipartite).

**Paper structure.** The structure moves systematically from defining the problem in sparse graph theory to introducing advanced tools like the cavity and replica methods, culminating in demonstrating their equivalence across various spectral observables.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

These lecture notes are based on the lectures on \emph{Statistical Mechanics of Random Matrices} delivered at the Spring College on the Physics of Complex Systems, held at the Abdus Salam International Centre for Theoretical Physics, Trieste, Italy, from 19 February to 15 March 2024. Their aim is to present a statistical-mechanics route to the spectral theory of sparse and diluted random matrices, with emphasis on cavity and replica methods, resolvent techniques, population dynamics, typical spectral densities, spectral-count fluctuations and large deviations, conditioned spectra, and non-Hermitian extensions. The written form of the notes has been deliberately expanded beyond the material actually covered during the lectures. This is partly because a set of lecture notes can afford a more systematic development than a sequence of blackboard lectures, and partly because several natural continuations of the material become clearer once the central methods have been introduced. Consequently, not every topic discussed here was presented during the College. The additional material is included to give a more coherent account of the subject and to indicate directions that, hopefully, can be covered in greater detail in future lectures or schools. Since these are lecture notes rather than a state-of-the-art review, the choice of topics is necessarily selective and is naturally tilted towards the author's own work and collaborations on this subject. I have nevertheless tried, within the limits of this format, to place the material in contact with the broader literature and to represent the surrounding state of the art as fairly as possible. Inevitably, some relevant contributions may be missing or treated too briefly; such omissions are unintentional and reflect the pedagogical scope of the notes rather than a judgement on their importance.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2606.09106"></a>
### [Dynamic scaling and Family-Vicsek universality in the Hubbard model at infinite temperature](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09106v1)

**Authors:** Cătălin Paşcu Moca, Doru Sticlet, Balázs Dóra  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.09106v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `analog quantum simulation` **3/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **3/5** · `non-equilibrium universality` **3/5** · `driven-dissipative phase transition` **2/5** · `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **1/5** · `entanglement & information structure` **1/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **1/5** · `scars & prethermalization` **1/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2606.09106_figures/2606.09106_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>

**Summary.** This theoretical study uses Quantum Generating Functions and Family-Vicsek scaling analysis to characterize how charge, spin, and energy fluctuations spread in the 1D Hubbard model. The key finding is that integrability dictates the long-time transport regime: integrable models exhibit anomalous (KPZ) or ballistic spreading, while breaking this symmetry drives the system towards classical diffusive behavior.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a powerful diagnostic tool (FV scaling) to map out the transition between different universality classes of transport (ballistic, KPZ, diffusive) in strongly correlated quantum systems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study investigates Family-Vicsek (FV) scaling of charge, spin, and energy fluctuations in the one-dimensional Hubbard model at infinite temperature.

**Main result.** The long-time dynamics are critically controlled by integrability: integrable cases show KPZ or ballistic transport for certain sectors, while breaking integrability leads to diffusive behavior across all measured quantities.

**Method.** The analysis employs a Quantum Generating Function (QGF) approach combined with Family-Vicsek scaling analysis to compute time-dependent cumulants of conserved quantities without reconstructing the full probability distribution.

**Model / system.** The system is the one-dimensional Fermi-Hubbard model, characterized by hopping ($J$) and onsite interaction ($U$). The Hamiltonian can be perturbed by adding a next-nearest-neighbor density interaction ($	ext{H}_{	ext{NNN}}$) to break integrability.

**Key observables.** Charge ($	ext{N}$), spin ($	ext{S}_z$), and energy ($	ext{E}$) fluctuations, quantified by their second cumulants ($\kappa^2$) and analyzed via the roughness function $W(\ell, t)$.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The key parameters are the interaction strength ($U$), the perturbation strength ($V$, related to $	ext{H}_{	ext{NNN}}$), and the system size/time scales.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The use of the QGF approach allows for the direct extraction of cumulants, bypassing the need to reconstruct the full quantum distribution function.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate Family-Vicsek scaling collapses for charge, spin, and energy roughness under different parameter regimes (e.g., $U=0$ vs $U
eq 0$, or $V=0$ vs $V 
eq 0$), confirming predicted power-law growth exponents.

**Paper structure.** The paper systematically analyzes the scaling behavior for charge, spin, and energy separately, contrasting the results obtained in the free limit ($U=0$), the interacting integrable case ($V=0$), and the non-integrable regime ($V 
eq 0$).

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We study Family-Vicsek scaling of charge, spin, and energy fluctuations in the one-dimensional Hubbard model at infinite temperature. Using a quantum generating function approach, we compute time-dependent cumulants of transferred conserved quantities and analyze how the corresponding roughness depends on subsystem size and time. We start by focusing on a single interface at half the chain and determine the transport exponents. Then we turn to fluctuations of a small finite interval and study the Family-Vicsek universality of fluctuations over an extended timescale. We find that the long-time scaling behavior is controlled by integrability. In the free limit, charge, spin, and energy all display ballistic transport. In the interacting integrable Hubbard chain, charge and spin cross over to a KPZ scaling regime, while the energy sector remains ballistic. Once integrability is broken by a next-nearest-neighbor density interaction, the long-time dynamics becomes diffusive in all sectors. In every case we also observe a short-time microscopic regime with apparently universal ballistic growth before the hydrodynamic scaling window sets in. The Family-Vicsek setup allows us to determine the growth, the saturation as well as the dynamical exponents.

</details>

### [Entanglement Generation through Coherent and Non-Coherent Control](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09599v1)

**Summary.** This paper investigates novel methods for creating quantum entanglement from unentangled initial states by exploiting coherent interference effects. It compares deterministic control using unitary path superposition against stochastic generation through noisy Pauli channels arranged in specific causal orders. The key finding is that the ability to interfere between alternative local operational pathways, even when decoherence is present, can serve as a powerful resource for generating robust entanglement.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a generalized theoretical framework showing that interference between local operations, rather than direct non-local interactions, can be the mechanism for generating and controlling quantum correlations, which is highly relevant to open quantum systems theory.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The central challenge is achieving controlled generation of quantum entanglement from initially separable states using local operations.

**Main result.** Entanglement can be deterministically generated by coherently superposing alternative operational paths, or stochastically through noisy channels, provided specific orthogonality conditions are met.

**Method.** The analysis uses mathematical frameworks involving coherent path superposition of local unitaries and the application of Pauli channels under both Path Superposition (PS) and Indefinite Causal Order (ICO).

**Model / system.** The system models a two-qubit state evolving under control mechanisms, analyzed using Bloch sphere representations for mixed states and focusing on Bell, GHZ, and W classes of entangled states.

**Key observables.** Concurrence ($C(ho)$), Purity ($	ext{Tr}ho_{	ext{out}}^2$), Success Probability ($P_{0}$), and the resulting entanglement class (Bell, GHZ, W).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Parameters governing local unitary operations ($U_k$) and Pauli channel strengths ($\alpha$ parameters) that dictate interference strength.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis often assumes starting from fully separable input states ($ho_1 \otimes ho_2$) and relies on post-selection measurements to reveal the entangled state.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate how local unitaries superposed along distinct paths generate entanglement (Fig 1); they also show $C(ho)$ vs. parameters for both coherent and non-coherent control schemes, demonstrating interference origins (Figs 3-6).

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from deterministic generation via unitary superposition to extending the analysis to noisy Pauli channels under PS/ICO architectures, culminating in a quantitative study of entanglement trade-offs using concurrence.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The controlled generation of quantum entanglement from separable states remains a central challenge in quantum information science. Here, we investigate entanglement generation using two related control paradigms: coherent path superposition of local unitary operations and stochastic implementations of Pauli channels under coherent control. We show that entangled states belonging to the Bell, GHZ and W classes, can be deterministically generated from fully separable inputs by coherently superposing alternative sets of local unitary transformations. Conditions on the local operators for entanglement generation are derived, and the resulting states are shown to be locally unitary equivalent to standard multipartite entangled states. We further extend the analysis to noisy scenarios, where separable mixed states evolve through pairs of Pauli channels arranged in path-superposition and indefinite causal order configurations. Closed-form expressions for the output states are obtained, and entanglement is quantified using concurrence. By exploring representative channel families across their parameter space, we identify regimes where stochastic entanglement emerges, determine the associated success probabilities, and characterize trade-offs between entanglement and purity.

</details>

**Summary.** The paper develops a non-Bloch topological framework to analyze magnon edge modes in 1D antiferromagnetic chains. It resolves the conflict between trivial bulk invariants and observable boundary states by defining a generalized winding number. This method successfully predicts that these edge modes are controllable via local perturbations, suggesting experimental realization using engineered magnetic nanostructures.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a rigorous theoretical tool (non-Bloch topology) to characterize localized excitations in magnetic systems where standard bulk band theory fails, offering insights applicable to engineered quantum materials.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study addresses the emergence of magnon edge modes in an antiferromagnetic spin chain when conventional Bloch topological invariants fail to predict their existence, indicating a breakdown of bulk-boundary correspondence.

**Main result.** A non-Bloch band theory utilizing a generalized Brillouin zone and a specialized winding number successfully predicts the presence and tunability of these boundary-localized magnon edge states.

**Method.** The authors employ linear spin-wave theory, constructing a pseudo-Hermitian dynamic matrix. They then develop a non-Bloch framework to calculate topological invariants by solving finite-difference equations on a generalized Brillouin zone.

**Model / system.** The system is a one-dimensional antiferromagnetic spin chain described by a Hamiltonian including Heisenberg exchange, Dzyaloshinskii–Moriya interaction (DMI), and easy-axis anisotropy. The analysis considers open boundary conditions (OBC) and tunable boundary perturbations.

**Key observables.** Magnon excitation spectrum eigenvalues, non-Bloch winding number ($W_{nB}$), Inverse Participation Ratio (IPR), and the energy levels of edge states as a function of boundary potential ($\epsilon$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** DMI strength ($D$), anisotropy ($\kappa$), exchange couplings ($J_1, J_2$), and the magnitude of the boundary perturbation ($\epsilon$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on linear spin-wave theory. For detailed winding number calculations, the DMI term is often set to zero ($D=0$) for analytical tractability.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the antiferromagnetic chain structure, energy spectra showing edge states appearing/disappearing with $\epsilon$, and plots of the non-Bloch winding number across the generalized Brillouin zone.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from establishing the failure of Bloch theory to defining the non-Bloch framework. It then applies this theory to calculate topological invariants, demonstrating how these invariants correctly track the appearance and disappearance of edge states controlled by boundary perturbations.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We define a winding number within the Non-Bloch band theory framework that captures the emergence of magnon edge modes in a one-dimensional antiferromagnetic spin chain, even when the conventional Bloch winding number is trivial. Within linear spin-wave theory, magnon excitations are governed by a non-Hermitian dynamic matrix, despite the underlying Hamiltonian being Hermitian. The symmetry classification of this matrix yields a trivial bulk invariant, however, finite systems exhibit boundary-localized modes, signaling a breakdown of the conventional bulk-boundary correspondence. We further show that these edge modes can be controlled via boundary perturbations. By tuning the boundary potential, the modes can be driven into or out of the bulk spectrum. To resolve the bulk-boundary mismatch, we develop a non-Bloch framework based on a generalized Brillouin zone and a winding number that correctly predicts the presence of edge states. Our results establish boundary-controlled topological transitions that are experimentally accessible through local Zeeman fields or modified edge anisotropy in antiferromagnetic van der Waals nanostructures.

</details>

**Summary.** The paper develops a comprehensive mathematical classification of Markovian open quantum channels acting on two-state systems (rebits). It then applies this theory to color science, demonstrating that the effect of non-neutral light sources causes chromatic distortion by reducing the distinguishability between colors, which is quantified using relative entropy.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a rigorous quantum information framework (Markovian dynamics) to model complex biological phenomena like color vision, bridging abstract quantum physics with sensory neuroscience.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper classifies Markovian dynamics for open two-state quantum systems (rebits) and applies this framework to model how illuminant changes cause chromatic distortion in human color perception.

**Main result.** A general structure for Markovian rebit channels is derived, and the theory successfully models chromatic distortion by showing that non-neutral illuminants induce a measurable reduction in color distinguishability quantified by quantum relative entropy.

**Method.** The authors use techniques from open quantum system theory, deriving structural properties of CPTP maps (channels) via the GKSL equation and applying these mathematical tools to psycho-physical models involving Lüders measurements.

**Model / system.** The physical system is a single-rebit open quantum system whose state evolves under Markovian dynamics. The model uses the Bloch vector representation for rebit states, $ho_s$, and relates channel evolution to established color vision models.

**Key observables.** Chromatic distinguishability (quantified by relative entropy $R$), the Bloch vector components $(s_1, s_2)$, and the stationary state $\mathbf{v}_{s0}$.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The decay rates ($\alpha_{i,j}$) derived from the Lindblad operators defining the illuminant channel; the Lorentz boost parameter $\gamma_e$.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes continuous differentiability of the channel components and models color perception using a rebit state space parameterized by real numbers.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate chromatic distortion caused by specific non-neutral illuminants (orange, green) and show how the level sets of relative entropy change over time, demonstrating reduced distinguishability.

**Paper structure.** The paper first tackles the abstract mathematical classification of Markovian rebit channels using semigroup properties. It then transitions to applying this theory to color perception, deriving specific channel forms for illuminants and dysfunction models, and finally quantifying distortion via relative entropy.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

This paper investigates the Markovian dynamics of open two-state quantum systems defined over the real numbers (rebits). Two main objectives are pursued. First, we present a comprehensive classification of Markovian rebit quantum channels, i.e. one-parameter semigroups of completely positive, trace-preserving (CPTP) maps acting on the rebit state space. We show that a full characterisation of their action can be achieved and that describing these channels as solutions of the GKSL equation allows us to explicitly identify the associated Lindblad generators and conditions for complete positivity. Second, we present an original application of this classification to colour perception. Using a recent model in which perceived colours arise from Lüders measurements on the rebit state space, we show how chromatic distortion induced by a non-neutral illuminant can be modelled by a Markovian rebit channel that progressively diminishes colour distinguishability. Other types of channels could be used to study colour vision deficiencies. These phenomena are illustrated by simulations on digital images, highlighting the relevance of rebit Markovian dynamics in modelling colour vision.

</details>

### [Fidelity susceptibility and geometric response in flux-tuned Dirac systems: exact results from a low-energy two-level reduction](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08280v1)

**Authors:** C. A. S. Almeida  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.08280v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `non-equilibrium universality` **3/5** · `driven-dissipative phase transition` **2/5** · `entanglement & information structure` **2/5** · `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **1/5** · `Kondo & dissipative impurity` **1/5** · `analog quantum simulation` **1/5** · `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **1/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **1/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **1/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2606.08280_figures/2606.08280_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>

(b) Normalized Bloch-sphere polar angle (2/π) arctan(ν/m) for the same masses. Since gλλ = 1 4(dθ/dν)2 (Sect. 7), the curvature of the ground-state trajectory on the Bloch sphere directly generates the Lorentzian profile in panel (a).</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2606.08280_figures/2606.08280_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 2: (a) Integrated Bures response χ(m) = π/(8m), representing the total geo- metric response of a single avoided crossing in the effective theory (Sect. 6). (b) Ge- ometric (paramagnetic) contribution 2gmax λλ = 1/(2m2) and diamagnetic contribution −∂2 λE− ν=0 = 1/m to the peak persistent current susceptibility at ν = 0, as functions of m. The shaded region marks the geometric excess; for m &lt; 1/2 the Bures metric dom- inates the total susceptibility.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This theoretical work analyzes the parametric sensitivity of flux-tuned Dirac fermions by employing the Bures metric. The authors derive an exact result showing that the integrated geometric susceptibility scales universally as $1/m$, mirroring critical divergences. This establishes a direct link between information geometry and measurable physical responses like persistent currents in mesoscopic systems.

**Why it may be interesting.** The connection between information geometry (Bures metric) and measurable transport properties like persistent currents provides a powerful, non-standard tool to characterize quantum critical behavior in condensed matter systems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study quantifies the parametric sensitivity of quantum systems using information geometry, specifically by analyzing the Bures metric (fidelity susceptibility) in flux-tuned Dirac fermions.

**Main result.** The integrated geometric susceptibility exhibits an exact universal Lorentzian profile with inverse-mass scaling ($\chi(m) \sim 1/m$), mirroring critical power-law divergences near gap closing.

**Method.** The authors use a controlled low-energy projection of the full Dirac Hamiltonian onto an effective two-level subspace to derive closed-form expressions for the Bures metric and its integral susceptibility.

**Model / system.** The system is modeled by 2D massive Dirac fermions subjected to an Aharonov-Bohm flux. The analysis is simplified using an effective two-level Hamiltonian, $H_{	ext{eff}}(
u) = 
u \sigma_x + m \sigma_z$, valid near integer flux.

**Key observables.** Bures metric (fidelity susceptibility), integrated geometric susceptibility $\chi(m)$, and the paramagnetic contribution to persistent current susceptibility.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Dirac mass ($m$), reduced Aharonov-Bohm flux ($\lambda$), and the chiral limit ($m 	o 0$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on a low-energy projection onto an effective two-level subspace, which is valid in the vicinity of integer flux values.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the Lorentzian peak of the Bures metric $g_{\lambda\lambda}(
u)$ near $
u=0$ and plot the integrated susceptibility $\chi(m) = \pi/(8m)$, comparing geometric vs. diamagnetic contributions to total susceptibility.

**Paper structure.** The paper first introduces the use of the Bures metric for parametric sensitivity in Dirac systems, derives an effective two-level Hamiltonian near integer flux, and then calculates the exact closed-form expressions for both the local (Lorentzian) and integrated ($\chi(m)$) geometric susceptibilities.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We study the parametric sensitivity of two-dimensional massive Dirac fermions subject to Aharonov-Bohm flux insertion, using the Bures metric (fidelity susceptibility) as the central statistical-mechanical response function. Near integer values of the reduced flux, the low-energy spectrum undergoes a flux-induced avoided crossing whose structure is controlled by the Dirac mass. Through a controlled low-energy projection of the full Dirac=Aharonov-Bohm operator onto an effective two-level subspace, valid near integer flux, we derive an exact closed-form expression for the ground-state Bures metric, which takes a universal Lorentzian profile centered at integer flux with width set by the mass parameter. The peak value scales as $g^{\max}_{λλ}\sim m^{-2}$, diverging in the chiral limit in direct analogy with the divergence of thermodynamic susceptibilities near critical points. We introduce an integrated geometric susceptibility $χ(m) = π/(8m)$, whose inverse-mass scaling is the information-geometric counterpart of power-law critical behavior, with the Dirac mass playing the role of a relevant coupling controlling the distance from the chiral fixed point. The Lorentzian profile is shown to arise from the curvature of the ground-state manifold on the Bloch sphere, requiring no dynamical input beyond the spectral structure. Importantly, this geometric response is independent of Berry curvature and topological invariants, emerging instead from a universal local spectral mechanism. Through its spectral representation, the Bures metric is identified as the geometric (paramagnetic) contribution to the persistent current susceptibility, encoding the sensitivity of persistent currents and orbital magnetization to flux variations and connecting information geometry to measurable response functions in mesoscopic Dirac systems.

</details>

**Authors:** A. A. Saharian, E. L. Karapetyan, G. V. Mirzoyan  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.09781v1>

**Summary.** This theoretical paper investigates quantum scalar fields in curved spacetimes containing topological defects like cosmic strings. By developing a general framework based on geometric decomposition, the authors solve the field equation using separation of variables. The ultimate goal is to calculate the vacuum correlation function (Hadamard two-point function) for these exotic physical setups.

**Why it may be interesting.** While focused on gravity/QFT, the mathematical techniques—solving separated differential equations with complex boundary conditions and deriving correlation functions—are highly relevant to open quantum systems and many-body physics where geometry or external fields modify standard Hamiltonians.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The research develops a general framework to study how topological defects influence quantum scalar fields propagating within warped spacetime geometries.

**Main result.** The authors derive explicit forms for the Hadamard two-point function in specific defect backgrounds, such as global monopoles and cosmic strings embedded in AdS spacetime.

**Method.** The methodology involves decomposing geometric tensors (like the Ricci tensor) and separating the resulting field equation into independent radial, angular, and warp-coordinate parts to solve for mode functions.

**Model / system.** The system is a massive scalar field propagating on a $(D+1)$-dimensional warped spacetime background that incorporates anisotropic deformations due to topological defects. The geometry is characterized by warp factors and generalized metrics.

**Key observables.** The Hadamard two-point function G(x, x'), the mode functions $\phi_\sigma(x)$, and components of the Ricci tensor ($R_{ii}$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Curvature coupling parameter ($\xi$), mass $m$, warp factor parameters, and angular deficit parameters ($\alpha_i$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes symmetries that allow for the separation of variables in the field equation; specific boundary conditions are used to determine mode quantization.

**Figures summary.** Not specified (Bibliography section provided).

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses by first establishing a general formalism for curvature decomposition, then applying this to separate the scalar field equation into solvable components, and finally calculating the two-point function for specific defect models like global monopoles in AdS.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We develop a general framework for investigating the influence of topological defects on the local characteristics of a quantum scalar field in a warped geometry background. The Ricci tensor and curvature scalar are decomposed into contributions from the warp factor, the radial geometry and the angular defect structure. For an arbitrary curvature coupling parameter, the field equation is separated into independent radial, angular and warp-coordinate parts. A complete set of normalized mode functions is obtained for general values of the angular deficit parameters. The general formalism is applied to several specific cases, such as conformally flat warped spacetimes, generalized cosmic strings, global monopoles and anti-de Sitter (AdS)-type warped geometries. The Hadamard two-point function is then evaluated for a global monopole in AdS spacetime using the obtained mode functions.

</details>

### [Inhomogeneous Coulomb Models Without Defects are Sick: Domain Wall Energies Scaling as Volume (not Boundary Area)](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08613v1)

**Authors:** Garry Goldstein  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.08613v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

**Summary.** This work demonstrates a fundamental pathology in Coulomb lattice models: domain walls cost energy proportional to system volume rather than just boundary area. The authors prove this using the six vertex model and show that even when macroscopic constraints are met, boundaries impose extensive free energy costs, highlighting the critical role of boundary conditions in these systems.

**Why it may be interesting.** While focused on condensed matter statistical mechanics, the concept of boundary-induced extensive energy costs is relevant for understanding finite-size effects in quantum simulations or open quantum systems where boundary conditions are crucial.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The authors investigate pathological behavior in Coulomb models where domain wall energies scale extensively with system volume rather than merely with boundary area, even when macroscopic constraints are satisfied.

**Main result.** Generic domain walls cost energy proportional to the system volume, and even boundaries satisfying $
abla \cdot B \cdot n = 0$ can impose an extensive free energy cost on the Helmholtz free energy.

**Method.** The analysis uses theoretical techniques applied to exactly solvable lattice models, comparing partition functions under different boundary conditions (periodic vs. domain wall).

**Model / system.** The study applies to general lattice systems obeying a divergence-free constraint ($
abla \cdot \langle B angle = 0$), with specific examples including the square lattice six vertex model, quantum dimers, and classical spin ice.

**Key observables.** Domain wall energy scaling (Volume vs. Area), Helmholtz free energy cost, monopole density, and partition functions for different boundary conditions.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The divergence-free constraint ($
abla \cdot \langle B angle = 0$), the ratio of ground state pseudo-magnetic fields across a domain wall ($\left\langle \mathbf{B}_{R}ightangle -\left\langle \mathbf{B}_{L}ightangle$), and temperature/coupling constants.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on coarse-graining variables $\langle B angle$ related to microscopic degrees of freedom, and the comparison between different boundary condition ensembles (periodic vs. domain wall).

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate non-vanishing divergence at interfaces, six vertex configurations for the model, and comparisons of partition functions under various boundary conditions.

**Paper structure.** The paper establishes the general problem using Coulomb models, analyzes specific examples like the six vertex model to demonstrate volume scaling pathologies, and concludes by showing that even seemingly compatible boundaries have thermodynamic consequences.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

In this work we show that coulomb models, ones that obey a $\nabla\cdot\left\langle \mathbf{B}\right\rangle =0$ divergence free constraint - for $\left\langle \mathbf{B}\right\rangle $ being some coarse grained variables related to the microscopic degrees of freedom of the lattice system - are sick without the inclusion of defects with $\nabla\cdot\mathbf{B}\neq0$. We show that for generic boundaries (ones where the fluxes of the pseudo-magnetic fields on the boundary do not cancel: $\left(\left\langle \mathbf{B}_{R}\right\rangle -\left\langle \mathbf{B}_{L}\right\rangle \right)\cdot\mathbf{n}\neq0$ - here $\mathbf{n}$ is the unit normal and $\left\langle \mathbf{B}_{R/L}\right\rangle $ are the two ground state pseudo-magnetic fields on either side of the domain wall) without the inclusion of defects, sharp domain walls (on the order of the width of a unit cell) between different phases of the system cost energy proportional to system size (not boundary area). We present several different examples of this phenomena in the square lattice six vertex model, in quantum dimers and in classical spin ice in the presence of magnetic fields. We also show by example that the condition $\left(\left\langle \mathbf{B}_{R}\right\rangle -\left\langle \mathbf{B}_{L}\right\rangle \right)\cdot\mathbf{n}=0$ is a necessary but not sufficient condition for the boundaries to be compatible - that is domain wall energy to scale with domain wall area and not system size. To further present the importance of boundary conditions in Coulomb systems we show that system boundaries, even ones that satisfy $\int_{\partial V}\mathbf{B}\cdot\mathbf{n}=0$, have thermodynamic consequences - that is there is a cost, extensive in system size, to the Helmholtz free energy.

</details>

The black dashed line represents A/µ ≈1.2 obtained from panel (a).</sub>

**Summary.** This work theoretically and numerically analyzes shear banding in amorphous solids, proposing that this mechanical failure mode is not a simple fracture but rather a nonlinear instability mediated by topological screening. By simulating atomic models under shear, the authors confirm that the finite width of the resulting shear band is quantitatively determined by this screening mechanism.

**Why it may be interesting.** While focused on condensed matter mechanics, the concept of an instability being governed by a 'screening' mechanism analogous to those studied in quantum field theory or plasma physics provides a rich analogy for understanding non-equilibrium phase transitions in complex many-body systems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates shear banding, a mechanical instability in amorphous solids, by developing and numerically testing a nonlinear theory that incorporates plastic deformations as topological charges.

**Main result.** Shear banding is fundamentally established as a screened soft-mode instability, where the finite localization scale of the band is governed by topological screening rather than classical elasticity.

**Method.** The authors combine theoretical derivation (using nonlinear Lagrangian methods) with extensive numerical simulations on atomic models to verify quantitative predictions for the shear band width and amplitude.

**Model / system.** The study uses amorphous solids simulated via molecular dynamics using various repulsive potentials (harmonic, Hertzian, WCA) under athermal quasistatic shearing conditions.

**Key observables.** Accumulated stress ($\Sigma_{xy}$) vs. strain ($\gamma$), displacement profiles ($d_x(y)$), quadrupolar charge density ($Q$), and the screening parameter ($\kappa_e^2$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The screening length ($\ell \sim 1/\kappa_e$) and nonlinear coefficients derived from the instability equations are crucial for characterizing the band.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis often relies on simplifying assumptions, such as fixing boundary conditions or using a 1D projection approximation for the displacement field to match theoretical models to simulations.

**Figures summary.** Figures show stress-strain curves exhibiting shear localization, spatial maps of charge densities concentrated in bands, and quantitative fits comparing simulated displacement profiles and screening parameters against theoretical predictions.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from establishing the general nonlinear theory for instability (soft mode analysis) to applying this framework numerically using atomic simulations across different potentials, culminating in a demonstration that topological screening dictates the finite width of the shear band.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Shear banding is a well-known and widespread instability in strained solids: under external strain, the deformation localizes along a line in two dimensions or a plane in three dimensions. Developing a proper theoretical description of this phenomenon is key to understanding mechanical failure in solid materials. Very recently, a nonlinear theory extending classical elasticity to include plastic deformations as topological charges was proposed, offering detailed predictions on the nature and consequences of the shear-banding instability. The theory derives a Hessian operator whose lowest eigenvalue vanishes at the onset of instability, and the corresponding critical eigenmode describes the displacement field across the shear band. The resulting soft mode possesses the selected localization scale and subsequently saturates into a finite-width shear band. The aim of this Letter is to examine this theory numerically, establishing the role of topological screening and nonlinear instability as the mechanisms governing shear banding during athermal quasistatic deformation. We show that the displacement profile around the shear band is directly determined by the screening parameter and the nonlinear coefficient, thereby quantitatively verifying the theoretical predictions. Our results demonstrate that shear banding differs fundamentally from fracture: it arises from a nonlinear instability of an elastic field screened by plastic deformations. This establishes topological screening as the essential mechanism governing shear banding in amorphous solids.

</details>

**Summary.** The paper introduces using Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) to accelerate Adaptive VQE by learning which quantum operators are most likely to improve the ground state energy. By predicting these operators from the system's interaction graph instead of exhaustively checking every possibility, it significantly reduces classical computational overhead while maintaining high accuracy.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work directly addresses the scaling bottleneck in quantum algorithms by replacing expensive physical measurements (gradient evaluations) with fast classical inference, a crucial step for applying VQE to large, complex systems relevant to AMO physics.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The primary challenge is mitigating the classical computational bottleneck associated with repeatedly scanning a large operator pool during adaptive quantum circuit construction in VQE.

**Main result.** A Graph Neural Network (GNN) policy can learn to predict the optimal next entangling operator, achieving energy errors competitive with full gradient-based methods while drastically reducing the required classical overhead.

**Method.** The authors reformulate adaptive operator selection as a graph decision problem, training a GNN on simulated gradient magnitudes derived from quantum state evolution. The trained GNN acts as an efficient shortlist generator for subsequent exact rescoring.

**Model / system.** The work is initially developed using disordered long-range spin chains with power-law decaying interactions ($J_{ij} \propto |r_i - r_j|^{-\alpha}$). It is later benchmarked on small molecular active spaces (LiH, BeH2) mapped to qubit Hamiltonians.

**Key observables.** Energy expectation value ($\Delta E$), Mean Rank of the selected operator, and computational scaling complexity relative to pool size $|E|$.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Interaction decay exponent $\alpha$, circuit depth $T$, and the number/size of candidate operators in the pool.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The core assumption is that the process of adaptive circuit construction contains a learnable, exploitable structure that can be approximated by graph message passing. The GNN acts as a shortlist generator rather than a perfect oracle replacement.

**Figures summary.** Figures compare energy error vs. depth for various selection strategies (Gradient Oracle vs. GNN vs. Heuristics) and show the computational scaling advantage of the GNN approach over full gradient evaluations.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem, details the graph representation of the Hamiltonian/operators, describes the GNN architecture trained on simulated gradients from spin chains, validates performance against heuristics, and concludes with transferability tests on molecular benchmarks.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Adaptive variational quantum algorithms like ADAPT-VQE construct tailored ansätze by iteratively selecting operators from a pool using gradient-based criteria. While this avoids oversized parameter spaces, repeatedly scanning the full pool incurs a classical cost that scales linearly with pool size-a major bottleneck for systems with long-range interactions or large operator sets. Here, we reformulate adaptive operator selection as a graph-based decision problem and introduce a graph neural network (GNN) policy that predicts the next entangling operator directly from the interaction graph and state-dependent observables. Training data are generated from exact simulations of disordered long-range spin chains, using gradient magnitudes as supervision signals. The learned policy accurately reproduces the dominant structure of the greedy gradient-based selection rule, significantly outperforming heuristics based solely on interaction strength. Integrated into a variational quantum eigensolver (VQE) workflow, this GNN-VQE approach achieves energy errors close to standard ADAPT-VQE while drastically reducing full-pool gradient evaluations. To test transferability beyond spin models, we evaluate the policy on small active-space molecular benchmarks (LiH and BeH_$2$). We find the GNN is highly effective as a shortlist generator: exact rescoring over just a few GNN-proposed candidates recovers near-oracle rollout behavior while searching only a small fraction of the pool. These results demonstrate that adaptive circuit construction contains learnable structure that can be exploited to accelerate variational quantum algorithms.

</details>

**Authors:** Xinning Wang, Bohdan Khromets, Zachery Merino, Jonathan Baugh  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.08874v1>

The confining potential is further controlled by a grounded screening gate and the tunnel gate voltage W1. Barrier gates B1, B2 are kept at 0 V throughout the simulations. All quantities in panel (a) are shown relative to a reference (idling) voltage point (V1, V2, W1) = (0.2765 V, 0.2765 V, 0 V). (b) Exchange coupling J as a function of the tunnel gate voltage W1 and the inter-dot detuning V1 −V2. In panel (b), the exchange coupling is evaluated relative to a reference voltage point (V1, V2, W1) = (0.2765 V, 0.2765 V, 0.1 V), highlighting the exponential dependence of the inter-dot exchange on tunnel gate voltage.</sub>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2606.08874_figures/2606.08874_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 3. Representative noisy voltage control pulse used to implement an RX(π/2) gate on qubit 1 in the two-qubit sim- ulation, while qubit 2 undergoes a simultaneous identity op- eration. The controls V1 and V2 denote plunger-gate voltages and W1 denotes the interdot barrier-gate voltage. The ideal waveform realizes the target gate operation, whereas the su- perimposed fluctuations correspond to sampled random tele- graph voltage noise. These noisy control voltages are mapped directly onto the time-dependent Hamiltonian through the voltage-dependent parameters gi(⃗V ) and Ji(⃗V ).</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

For each value of τ (with τ = ⟨τ0⟩= ⟨τ1⟩), the charge-noise signal on a given gate is constructed as a normalized sum of Nrtn = 50 independent RTN realizations [Eq. (4)]. The time series extend beyond the single-gate duration tg = 10 µs and span the timescale of a simulated variational single-shot experiment, texp = 130 µs; only the first 50 µs are shown for clarity.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper rigorously analyzes how gate-voltage noise impacts silicon spin qubits used in variational quantum eigensolvers. By developing a co-simulation framework, the authors quantify that two-qubit gates are highly susceptible to voltage noise, while also demonstrating that advanced error mitigation techniques can partially recover fidelity when noise dynamics are characterized by their switching time.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides crucial, quantitative design rules for realizing scalable quantum hardware by directly linking device-level noise sources (voltage fluctuations) to algorithmic performance metrics (VQE accuracy).

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study assesses how hardware imperfections, specifically gate-voltage noise (static miscalibration and dynamic charge noise), degrade the performance of Variational Quantum Eigensolvers (VQE) when applied to silicon spin qubits.

**Main result.** Two-qubit gates are significantly more sensitive to voltage noise than single-qubit rotations due to the nonlinear dependence of exchange coupling on gate voltages. Furthermore, unitary error correction can partially mitigate noise effects if the noise switching time is short relative to the gate duration.

**Method.** The authors developed a hardware-algorithm co-simulation framework combining 3D electrostatics for parameter extraction with quantum process tomography (QPT) and Lindblad master equation simulations to model noisy gate operations.

**Model / system.** The system is silicon spin qubits in double quantum dots, whose dynamics are governed by an effective spin Hamiltonian derived from solving the Schrödinger equation using parameters ($\delta g$, $J$) extracted via Stark-shift models. Noise is modeled as Random Telegraph Noise (RTN) or systematic voltage scaling/offset errors.

**Key observables.** Gate fidelities ($F$), reconstructed noise channels ($\chi_{	ext{err}}$), coherence angle ($\Theta$), and the achievable chemical accuracy for ground-state energy estimation in VQE.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Noise amplitude ($A$), switching time ($	au$), gate duration ($t_g$), and voltage miscalibration parameters ($\sigma_a, \sigma_b$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on an effective-potential approximation for electrostatics and treats noise sources (like RTN) as independent or correlated stochastic processes.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the dependence of gate infidelity on miscalibration strength ($\sigma_a, \sigma_b$) and the crossover behavior of fidelity versus noise switching time ($	au/t_g$).

**Paper structure.** The paper systematically develops from modeling the physical hardware (electrostatics $	o$ effective Hamiltonian) to implementing noise models (static vs. dynamic RTN), benchmarking key gates ($	ext{RX}, \sqrt{	ext{SWAP}}, 	ext{CZ}$), and finally applying these diagnostics within the VQE circuit context.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Quantum computers offer a route to outperform classical methods in tasks such as molecular simulation, motivating hybrid algorithms like the Variational Quantum Eigensolver (VQE) for near-term devices. Silicon spin qubits are a promising platform for scalable quantum computation, but their performance is limited by hardware imperfections -- most notably charge-noise-induced potential fluctuations and static miscalibration of gate-electrode voltages -- which degrade quantum gate fidelities and, ultimately, algorithmic accuracy. Here we develop a hardware-algorithm co-simulation framework for silicon quantum-dot processors that links 3D electrostatics to effective $g$-factors and exchange couplings, and propagates voltage-level noise through realistic control pulses. Using VQE for $\mathrm{H}_2$ ground-state energy estimation as a circuit-level testbed, we study both static scaling/offset errors on the gate-electrode voltages and stochastic fluctuations modeled as random-telegraph noise with tunable amplitudes and switching times. At the gate level, we show that exchange-based two-qubit gates are roughly an order of magnitude more sensitive to these types of noise than ESR-driven single-qubit rotations. Quantum process tomography and Kraus-operator analysis further distinguish coherent and incoherent contributions and quantify the fraction of error that is, in principle, correctable by a compensating unitary. Embedding these noise models into the VQE circuit, we identify regimes of miscalibration strength and noise switching time compatible with chemically accurate energy estimates, and discuss how statistical post-processing based on the full distribution of noisy energy estimates could further improve accuracy.

</details>

### [Dynamical cavity method for continuous-time complex systems on sparse random graphs](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08689v1)

**Authors:** Fernando L. Metz, Isaac Pérez Castillo  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.08689v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **3/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **3/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5** · `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **1/5** · `driven-dissipative phase transition` **1/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2606.08689_figures/2606.08689_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>

The root history is generated from neighbour histories one time layer shorter; each neighbour history is generated from its own descendants with one further time layer removed. The recursion terminates at leaves drawn from the initial distribu- tion. The schematic is drawn for the zero-noise, constant-coupling additive-input example used to make the causal structure explicit. In the general pairwise formu- lation, ordinary source histories are replaced by the corresponding imposed-history branch path laws.</sub>

</details>

<img src="2606.08689_figures/2606.08689_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>

Coupling strengths are sampled from a Gaussian distribution with unit mean and unit variance. The time-discretization parameter is ∆= 0.1. Crosses denote direct finite-graph simulations of the discretized microscopic dynamics with N = 15000, while the remaining symbols denote the finite-depth population- dynamics construction with population size Npop = 105. The left panel shows the recurrent neural-network model and the right panel shows the Lotka–Volterra model with immigration rate λ = 10−2. Error bars are not shown.</sub>

The left panel shows the recurrent neural-network model and the right panel shows the Lotka–Volterra model with immigration rate λ = 10−2. Error bars are not shown.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2606.08689_figures/2606.08689_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 4: Graph-level view of the moving-window approximation. In the exact finite graph, the history window of a root i advances while the same rooted marked neigh- bourhood Ei is retained. In the moving-window population closure, the particle label a carries the shifted window, but the local marked environment used to generate the next endpoint is sampled anew. Thus the approximation is not in the one-step up- date, but in replacing the joint law of a window and its persistent rooted environment by a marginal window law together with an annealed environment draw.</sub>

</details>
