The terms Xu live on the vertices and ZuZv on the edges of H. Its frustration graph is the subdivision graph of the interaction graph.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper develops an invariant-based mathematical framework to characterize the dynamics generated by Pauli strings in quantum systems. By connecting Pauli Lie algebras to transvection groups using symplectic properties, it provides efficient algorithms to determine system symmetries and computational reachability. This unified approach is vital for analyzing structured quantum circuits used in variational algorithms and many-body physics.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a powerful algebraic toolkit (invariant-based classification) to analyze the computational power of quantum circuits defined by local interactions, which is crucial for understanding resource limitations in quantum computation.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The core problem is understanding the dynamical properties, reachability, and computational power of quantum systems generated by sets of Pauli strings.

**Main result.** The authors establish a unified, invariant-based framework connecting Pauli Lie algebras to subgroups of the Clifford group via symplectic properties, allowing for efficient classification algorithms.

**Method.** The methodology heavily relies on representation theory, Lie theory, and graph theory (specifically using frustration and commutator graphs) to analyze algebraic structures over $\mathbb{F}_2$.

**Model / system.** The focus is on quantum dynamics generated by Pauli strings ($\{I, X, Y, Z\}^{\otimes n}$), which models restricted quantum computation, variational algorithms, and certain many-body interactions.

**Key observables.** Pauli orbits, invariant subspaces, the structure of the associated Lie algebra, and whether the generators provide 3-designs for the Pauli Lie group.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The system size $n$ (number of qubits) and the specific generating set of Paulis are key parameters determining the complexity and structure.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis is restricted to the setting where generators are Pauli strings, which simplifies the problem from general quantum dynamics to one solvable with polynomial-time algorithms based on invariants.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate graph formalisms, specifically comparing frustration graphs and commutator graphs for a given generating set of Paulis.

**Paper structure.** The paper is highly structured across multiple parts, moving from an overview to detailed framework development using Pauli strings, invariant theory, Clifford groups, and finally illustrating applications in VQAs and many-body systems.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Understanding the dynamical properties of quantum systems is an essential task in quantum computing, quantum control, and many-body physics. Tools such as representation theory and Lie theory provide crucial information on reachability and computational power. However, this information can be difficult to access exactly or compute efficiently for arbitrary generating sets. Here we focus on the setting of Pauli strings, which satisfy numerous exceptional properties that simplify the problem. We find deep connections between Pauli Lie algebras and certain subgroups of the Clifford group generated by transvections, through the symplectic properties of the Pauli strings. This allows us to give an invariant-based perspective on these objects and their reachability, in the language of Pauli orbits, symmetries, and invariant subspaces. The invariant-based approach provides efficient algorithms for identifying Lie algebras and orbits, as well as a simple framework for analyzing structured Pauli generating sets. We also show in an elementary way that Clifford subgroups generated by transvections provide 3-designs for the corresponding Pauli Lie groups. We illustrate the framework through structured examples from variational quantum algorithms, restricted quantum computation, many-body systems, and random circuits.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2606.08690"></a>
### [High Precision Qubit-Efficient Variational Continuous Optimization via Amplitude Estimation](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08690v1)

**Authors:** Parth Danve  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.08690v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `analog quantum simulation` **1/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **1/5** · `quantum measurements` **1/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2606.08690_figures/2606.08690_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 1. Standard discretized variational circuit for n continuous decision variables. Each variable xi is encoded into a dedicated register of bi qubits as in (1), and a joint variational ansatz V (ϕ) acts across all registers. Measurement bitstrings are decoded via (2) and passed to a classical optimizer which updates ϕ iteratively. Total qubit count grows as Pn i=1 bi, increasing logarithmically with the required precision 1/ηi per variable.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2606.08690_figures/2606.08690_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 2. Proposed amplitude-based variational circuit for n continuous decision variables. Each variable xi is encoded into the measurement probability pi of a single qubit as in (3). The readout block estimates pi; the choice of readout procedure and its precision scaling are discussed in Section IV. Estimated probabilities ˆpi ≈pi are decoded classically using a decoding map as xi = g(ˆpi) and passed to a classical optimizer which updates ϕ iteratively.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2606.08690_figures/2606.08690_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 3. IQAE circuit for qubit i in round k. The oracle Ai = V (ϕ)|i prepares |ψi(ϕ)⟩, followed by mk applications of Qi from (6). Measurement outcomes update the confidence interval for pi = sin2(θi).</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper presents a novel variational framework for continuous optimization on standard quantum hardware by encoding variables into single-qubit measurement amplitudes rather than using binary discretization. By employing iterative Quantum Amplitude Estimation (IQAE) for readout, the method achieves superior resource scaling—linear in qubits and quadratically better in circuit depth compared to discretized approaches—thereby enabling high-precision computation without prohibitive hardware overheads.

**Why it may be interesting.** While focused on quantum computation theory, the efficiency gains in scaling resources ($\Theta(n)$ qubits vs. $\Theta(n \log(\dots))$) are crucial for any physical implementation, including those involving complex Hamiltonian simulation or optimization routines relevant to AMO physics.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The challenge of performing continuous optimization on standard gate-based quantum computers without resorting to resource-intensive discretization.

**Main result.** The proposed amplitude-based framework achieves a qubit cost scaling linearly with the number of variables ($\Theta(n)$) while improving readout complexity from quadratic ($O(\epsilon^{-2})$) to linear ($O(\epsilon^{-1})$) in estimation error, outperforming discretized methods significantly.

**Method.** It encodes each continuous variable into the measurement probability (squared amplitude) of a single qubit and uses iterative Quantum Amplitude Estimation (IQAE) for high-precision readout, bypassing explicit binary encoding.

**Model / system.** The work is situated within the standard gate-based quantum circuit model, applying variational algorithms to minimize objective functions $f(\mathbf{x})$ defined over real variables $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$.

**Key observables.** Objective function value error ($\delta_F$), variable precision ($\eta$ or $\epsilon$), and the number of required qubits.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The ratio between desired objective precision ($\delta_F$) and the resource scaling factors ($n, K, R$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The primary decoding map is assumed to be a bounded affine map; the objective function $f$ must be $K$-Lipschitz continuous with respect to the $\ell_\infty$-norm.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the comparison between discretized and amplitude-based variational circuits, detailing the structure of the single-qubit encoding and the adaptive IQAE readout procedure for multiple variables.

**Paper structure.** The paper first establishes the limitations of traditional discretization. It then introduces the qubit-efficient amplitude-encoding framework, details the superior IQAE readout technique, and concludes by providing a rigorous resource comparison (Table II & III) against the discretized baseline across both qubit count and circuit execution complexity.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Optimization of continuous-variable objectives on standard gate-based quantum computers via variational algorithms such as QAOA is typically approached by first discretizing each decision variable into a finite binary representation. This increases qubit requirements and restricts solution precision through fixed-resolution encodings. We propose a qubit-efficient variational framework for continuous optimization that instead encodes each decision variable into the squared amplitude or equivalently, the measurement probability of a single qubit state. This removes explicit discretization from the variable representation while remaining entirely within the standard qubit circuit model unlike methods like CV-QAOA employing qumode based hardware to achieve the same. To read out encoded variables, we propose using amplitude estimation rather than naive sampling or tomographic reconstruction, with the goal of improving precision scaling for continuous-value recovery. We outline how amplitude-estimation error propagates to decision-variable error and then to objective-value error under standard regularity assumptions, suggesting a distinct width-versus-precision tradeoff relative to discretized approaches. In particular, the framework replaces the logarithmic increase in qubits needed for finer binary precision with a constant cost of one qubit per decision variable, while shifting accuracy requirements into the estimation procedure. We position this approach relative to traditional discretized variational formulations, and argue that it provides a promising new direction for continuous optimization on standard qubit architectures.

</details>

### [Learning Dynamics Reveal a Hierarchy of Weight-Induced Layerwise Gram Metrics](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09744v1)

**Authors:** Claudio Nordio  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.09744v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

**Summary.** This theoretical paper reformulates gradient descent dynamics for deep neural networks by moving the description from weight space to activation fields. It discovers that as network depth increases, maintaining a closed dynamical description requires introducing an escalating hierarchy of geometric operators called pullback Gram metrics. This suggests that the complexity of learning is fundamentally governed by the geometry of information transport across layers.

**Why it may be interesting.** The formal mathematical machinery developed—identifying geometric operators (Gram metrics) required to close a dynamical system—is highly analogous to techniques used in characterizing complex many-body systems or effective Hamiltonians in quantum field theory.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The goal is to reformulate gradient descent dynamics in deep neural networks from a weight-space evolution into a closed collective dynamics defined entirely on the training-set activation fields.

**Main result.** For deep networks, the dynamical closure requires introducing a hierarchy of 'pullback Gram metrics' ($G_{\alphaeta}^{\ell}$) that mediate information transport across layers. This shows that network complexity is encoded in the geometry of these transport operators.

**Method.** The authors derive recursive equations for activation fields, conjugate fields, and residuals by analyzing gradient descent updates layer-by-layer, leading to a structured kernel decomposition.

**Model / system.** The model is a feed-forward ReLU network trained using quadratic loss. The analysis progresses from single hidden layers to deep architectures ($L$ layers), requiring the introduction of auxiliary fields (conjugate and Gram metrics) to maintain mathematical closure.

**Key observables.** Activation fields ($u^\alpha_l$), Conjugate Fields ($b^\alpha_l$), Residuals ($r^\alpha$), and the hierarchy of Pullback Gram Metrics ($G_{\alphaeta}^{\ell}$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The depth of the network ($L$) is the primary varying parameter, as it dictates the required complexity of the closure mechanism.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes fixed readout and restricts the dynamics to first-order approximations in the learning rate ($\eta$), neglecting threshold-crossing events.

**Figures summary.** Not specified by notes; the structure suggests showing the increasing complexity (number of required metrics) as depth increases.

**Paper structure.** The paper builds incrementally: starting with a single hidden layer to establish closure via a factorized kernel, extending to two layers using overlaps, and finally demonstrating that achieving closure for $L \ge 3$ necessitates defining and tracking the hierarchical pullback Gram metrics ($G_{\alphaeta}^{\ell}$).

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We study feed-forward ReLU networks with fixed readout and quadratic loss. The aim is to rewrite gradient descent not primarily as a dynamics in weight space, but as a collective dynamics closed in terms of fields defined on the training-set space. For a single hidden layer, the weight variables can be eliminated from the activation dynamics, yielding a closed equation for the residuals governed by a collective kernel that factorizes into an input-geometric matrix and a dynamical co-activation matrix. For deeper networks, the residual dynamics retains a clean layer-wise kernel structure. However, from depth three onward, closure requires a hierarchy of weight-induced Gram operators that mediate information transport across layers.

</details>

**Summary.** This work uses ab initio calculations on $	ext{HgBa}_2	ext{Ca}_2	ext{Cu}_3	ext{O}_8$ to reveal that the mechanism for high-temperature superconductivity involves an emergent local attraction derived from strong Coulomb repulsion. The study successfully models the pressure dependence of $T_c$, showing it results from a complex interplay between hopping, onsite repulsion ($U$), and off-site repulsion ($V$). This provides a new theoretical route for designing superconducting materials.

**Why it may be interesting.** The identification of pairing via 'attraction from reduced repulsion' provides a novel theoretical framework for understanding high-$T_c$ superconductivity, linking it directly to correlation effects modulated by external pressure.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The primary goal is to elucidate the microscopic mechanism responsible for high-temperature superconductivity (HTSC) in cuprate superconductors, specifically focusing on $	ext{HgBa}_2	ext{Ca}_2	ext{Cu}_3	ext{O}_8$ ($	ext{Hg1223}$).

**Main result.** The pairing mechanism is identified as an emergent local attraction generated counter-intuitively from the strong bare local Coulomb repulsion ($U$), which contrasts with conventional BCS theory. The pressure dependence of $T_c$ is successfully modeled by the interplay between increased hopping ($t$), decreased $U$, and reduced off-site repulsion ($V$).

**Method.** The study employs ab initio calculations solving low-energy effective Hamiltonians using advanced variational solvers supplemented by neural networks, alongside Variational Monte Carlo (VMC) methods.

**Model / system.** The system is the triple-layer cuprate $	ext{Hg1223}$, modeled via an effective Hamiltonian incorporating onsite ($U$) and nearest-neighbor off-site ($V$) Coulomb interactions. The analysis distinguishes between inner ($	ext{IP}$) and outer ($	ext{OP}$) superconducting planes.

**Key observables.** Superconducting critical temperature ($T_c$), $d$-wave SC order parameter, spin structure factors ($S_s(q)$), and the effective attraction derived from onsite repulsion energy curvature.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Pressure (0 GPa to 60 GPa), doping concentration ($\delta$), hopping parameters ($t$), and Coulomb interactions ($U$, $V$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The methodology relies on extrapolating calculated properties to the thermodynamic limit ($L 	o \infty$) and assumes that the pairing mechanism can be captured by analyzing the energy dependence of onsite repulsion.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the dome-like pressure dependence of $T_c$ for $	ext{Hg1223}$, show the coexistence of SC and AF orders in different layers, and compare theoretical $T_c$ predictions against experimental data under applied pressure.

**Paper structure.** The paper systematically analyzes the Hamiltonian parameters ($t, U, V$) as a function of pressure. It first establishes the strong correlation origin of HTSC, then details how pressure modifies these interactions, leading to an emergent attraction that drives superconductivity, while also confirming the coexistence of SC and AF orders.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Triple-layer cuprate superconductor $\mathrm{HgBa_2Ca_2Cu_3O_8}$ (Hg1223) keeps the record of the highest superconducting (SC) critical temperature $T_{c}\sim 134$K among all the existing materials at ambient pressure. $T_{c}$ further increases under pressure up to $T_{c}\sim 160$K. However, its microscopic mechanism remains to be elucidated. We solve {\it ab initio} Hamiltonians for Hg1223 using a variational solver supplemented by a neural network. The pressure dependence of the $d$-wave SC order parameter and estimated $T_{c}$ show a dome-like structure in essential agreement with the experimental indications. The origin of the strong SC amplitude at ambient pressure is identified as strong local Coulomb repulsion $U$ attributed to poor screening. Further increase in $T_{c}$ under pressure is understood from interplay of three elements, namely increased electron hopping $t$, decreased $U$ and more importantly, strongly reduced offsite Coulomb repulsion $V$ with increasing pressure. Pairing mechanism is identified as the emergent local attraction counterintuitively generated from the originally strong local repulsion $U$. The emergent attraction is interpreted from ``attraction from reduced repulsion'', originating from the release of the fluctuating doubly-occupied sites characterized from the ``false vacuum'' in the Mott insulator to the double-occupation-free $d$-wave SC states upon carrier doping. This instantaneous attraction is in contrast with the conventional BCS SC mediated by bosonic glues. The local attraction is consistent with the electron fractionalization supported in experimental analyses. The coexistence of the SC and antiferromagnetic order is also demonstrated as a characteristic feature of the multi-layer system. The microscopic understanding of Hg1223 offers a new route explicitly using this emergent attraction to design and optimize SC materials.

</details>

<a id="paper-2606.09723"></a>
### [A Bell-State Extension of Loop-Back Quantum Key Distribution](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09723v1)

**Authors:** Luis Adrián Lizama-Pérez  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.09723v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `entanglement & information structure` **1/5** · `quantum measurements` **1/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2606.09723_figures/2606.09723_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 1: Compact flow diagram of the Loop-Back Bell-State QKD protocol with full-Pauli encoding. The numbered blocks correspond to Steps (1)–(6) described in Section 2. Alice privately prepares the initial Bell state |χ0⟩and sends one qubit to Bob. Bob applies a local Pauli operation and returns the qubit through the same quantum link. Alice performs a Bell- state measurement, deterministically infers the applied operation, and uses a subset of rounds for verification and key generation. The fact that χ0 is chosen and known exclusively by Alice reinforces the indistinguishability of the induced transition for any external observer.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** The paper introduces an advanced Bell-state extension to the Loop-Back Quantum Key Distribution protocol. By encoding information in entangled Bell states and leveraging a hidden initial reference state known only to Alice, it achieves superior security against eavesdropping and significantly boosts key generation efficiency compared to prior methods. This makes the scheme highly suitable for real-world, lossy quantum communication links.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work directly advances quantum cryptography protocols, providing a theoretically robust framework for practical QKD implementations suitable for challenging environments like mobile or edge nodes where high loss is expected.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses persistent security and efficiency challenges in bidirectional Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) protocols, particularly those based on the Loop-Back architecture.

**Main result.** A Bell-state extension of the Loop-Back QKD protocol is proposed that enhances detectability against attacks to $\approx 3/4$ per round and increases effective throughput up to $50\%$ by utilizing entangled states.

**Method.** The scheme uses entangled Bell states, where Alice privately knows an initial reference state $|\chi_0angle$. Bob applies a local Pauli operation ($U_B$), and Alice performs a Bell-state measurement (BSM) to deterministically infer $U_B$ without basis sifting.

**Model / system.** The system is based on maximally entangled Bell states ($\{|\Phi^\pmangle, |\Psi^\pmangle\}$). The protocol operates by encoding information through the full four-dimensional Bell basis, contrasting with previous two-dimensional subspace restrictions.

**Key observables.** Intrinsic detection probability ($P_d \approx 3/4$); Effective throughput (bounded by $p_{BSM}$); Deterministic inference of Bob's Pauli operation ($U_B$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The initial private Bell state $|\chi_0angle$; The success probability of the BSM ($p_{BSM}$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Security critically relies on Alice privately knowing $\chi_0$, which prevents adversaries from reconstructing the encoding mapping. The traveling subsystem is assumed to remain locally maximally mixed.

**Figures summary.** The notes reference a flow diagram (Figure 1) illustrating the six sequential steps of the protocol, and Table 1 summarizes how local Pauli operations act on the four Bell states.

**Paper structure.** The paper builds by first identifying limitations in existing QKD protocols. It then introduces the Bell-state extension using entangled states to overcome subspace confinement. Security is established by showing that only Alice, knowing $\chi_0$, can deterministically map Bob's action via BSM, leading to enhanced detectability and efficiency.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Bidirectional quantum key distribution (QKD) protocols face persistent challenges related to classical disclosure, confinement of the signal space to predictable subspaces, and limited detectability under substitution or entanglement-swapping attacks. In this work, we present a Bell-state extension of the Loop-Back QKD architecture that improves efficiency and detectability while preserving its defining feature of a simplified, measurement-free remote terminal. The protocol employs entangled Bell states together with deterministic local Pauli encoding at the remote node. A central element is that Alice privately prepares and knows the initial Bell state, which serves as a hidden reference enabling her to interpret the Bell-state transition induced by Bob, while preventing an adversary from reconstructing the encoding without access to this reference. By exploiting both intra- and inter-family Bell transitions, the scheme expands the effective signal space beyond the subspace restrictions of earlier two-way protocols. Alice performs a Bell-state measurement to deterministically infer Bob's operation without any basis sifting. Although the traveling subsystem remains locally maximally mixed, concealing the initial Bell family amplifies disturbance under separable substitution strategies, yielding an intrinsic detection probability of approximately 3/4 per round. From an efficiency perspective, the protocol lifts the intrinsic post-selection limitation of single-qubit Loop-Back schemes: the effective throughput is bounded only by the Bell-state measurement success probability, reaching up to 50% in linear-optical implementations. These features make the proposed scheme particularly suitable for mobile or edge-based QKD scenarios, where passive remote nodes must operate under high loss and limited interaction times.

</details>

<a id="paper-2606.09791"></a>
### [Certified spectral functions from lattice Monte Carlo data](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09791v1)

**Authors:** Sophie Mutzel, Antoine Tilloy  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** numerical methods · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.09791v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **1/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **1/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2606.09791_figures/2606.09791_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1. Middle: Schematic projection of the convex space of admissible positive spectral densities. Left: Approximation by discretizing in ω, which is fine for all practical purposes, if a large number of variables Nv for discretization in ω is taken. The resulting bounds approach the true ones from inside as the number of grid points Nv grows, but are not rigorous at finite Nv (orange). Right: Rigorously bounding the K¨all´en-Lehmann kernel by piecewise constant (turquoise) or piecewise affine (dark blue) envelopes enlarges the feasible set. The resulting bounds approach the true ones from outside as the number of envelope pieces grows, and are rigorous at every finite Nv.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2606.09791_figures/2606.09791_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 2. Rigorous piecewise constant (turquoise) and piece- wise affine (dark blue) upper and lower envelopes f ± j (ω) for the lattice kernel (6) of the K¨all´en-Lehmann relation at one representative tj. Convexity of K(ω, tj) on (0, ∞) ensures that secants provide rigorous upper and tangents provide rig- orous lower bounds.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2606.09791_figures/2606.09791_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 3. Convergence with the number of constant or affine basis functions Nv for the envelopes f ± of the kernel K(ω, t). We used 104 configurations for ϕ4-theory at λ = 4 · 10−3 and g = 2, on a 256 × 256 lattice. We also show the bounds obtained by solving the primal SDP (12) discretized on a grid with Nv discretization points.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2606.09791_figures/2606.09791_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 4. Rigorous upper and lower bounds on the smeared spectral density (σ2 = 0.2) from the dual SDP (26) for ϕ4- theory at λ = 4 · 10−3 and g = 2, on a 256 × 256 lattice. The symmetrized correlator (4) provides Nc = 129 constraints. The vertical dashed line corresponds to the mass-gap esti- mate, obtained from an effective-mass fit.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper presents a rigorous mathematical framework to extract spectral information ($\rho(\omega)$) from noisy lattice Monte Carlo measurements. It transforms the difficult inverse problem into a sequence of solvable Semidefinite Programs (SDPs) by constructing bounding envelopes for the underlying kernel functions. This yields mathematically guaranteed upper and lower bounds on physical observables, providing both quantitative results and a powerful consistency check for the input data.

**Why it may be interesting.** While focused on lattice QFT, the methodology—transforming an infinite-dimensional inverse problem into a sequence of solvable, rigorously bounded SDPs—is highly relevant to any field involving ill-posed reconstruction from noisy data (e.g., spectral analysis in quantum dynamics).

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The core challenge is rigorously recovering spectral observables, such as the spectral density $\rho(\omega)$, from Euclidean correlation functions measured via lattice Monte Carlo simulations.

**Main result.** The authors developed a method to derive rigorous upper and lower bounds on arbitrary linear functionals of $\rho(\omega)$ by relaxing the problem into solvable Semidefinite Programs (SDPs).

**Method.** The approach reformulates the ill-posed inverse problem as a convex optimization problem, which is then rigorously relaxed into a hierarchy of finite SDPs using bounding envelopes for the kernel function.

**Model / system.** The work applies to lattice quantum field theory, demonstrated specifically on two-dimensional $\phi^4$ theory. The analysis relies on relating measured correlation functions $C(t)$ to the spectral density via a lattice Källén-Lehmann representation.

**Key observables.** Spectral density $\rho(\omega)$, Euclidean correlation functions $C(t)$, and linear functionals of $\rho(\omega)$.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The covariance matrix $\Sigma$ derived from Monte Carlo data, the maximum allowed deviation $F_{\max}$, and the lattice spacing $a$.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Assumes that the underlying physics is governed by a reflection positive action and that statistical errors are approximately Gaussian-distributed. The method relies on constructing bounding envelopes for continuous functions.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the convergence of bounds using piecewise constant versus piecewise affine basis sets, showing how these envelopes provide rigorous upper and lower bounds on the spectral density as the number of basis functions increases.

**Paper structure.** The paper builds from identifying the ill-posed nature of the problem to formulating it as a convex optimization problem. It then details the mathematical machinery of Lagrange duality and relaxation techniques, culminating in solving the resulting finite SDPs for rigorous bounds.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The Monte Carlo method, applied to lattice quantum field theory, gives access to Euclidean correlation functions with well-understood error bars. Recovering the observables one cares about, such as the spectral density, requires solving an ill-posed inverse problem, usually tackled with heuristics that lose rigorous control of the error. Instead of trying to find the ``best'' spectral density $ρ(ω)$, we ask how small or large linear functionals $\int_{\mathbb{R}^+} G(ω) ρ(ω) \mathrm{d} ω$ of it can be, given the Monte Carlo data and the reflection positivity of the lattice action. This is a convex but infinite-dimensional problem. We show how its dual can be rigorously relaxed into a hierarchy of finite semidefinite programs, solvable with standard solvers and enjoying strong convergence guarantees. The resulting bounds are rigorous even when the relaxation is not tight, and converge quickly to the regime where the error is entirely dominated by Monte Carlo statistics. The method also flags implausible Monte Carlo data, for instance underestimated error bars, through an infeasibility certificate. We demonstrate it on lattice $φ^4$ theory in two dimensions.

</details>

<img src="2606.08916_figures/2606.08916_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 4. Magnetic-field dependence of the magnetization for Gd1−xErxVO4. (a–d) Isothermal magnetization curves M(µ0H) measured at low temperatures (2–30 K) with magnetic fields increasing from 0 to 7 T and then decreasing from 7 to 0 T. (e–h) Corresponding smoothed derivatives dM/d(µ0H), highlighting the spin-flop-like field-induced anomaly. The anomaly observed in GdVO4 becomes progressively weaker with increasing Er substitution. Raw data plots are provided in Fig. S1 of the Supplemental Material [41].</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2606.08916_figures/2606.08916_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 5. Arrott-type plots (µ0H/M versus M 2) for Gd1−xErxVO4 measured between 2 and 10 K. (a) x = 0, (b) x = 0.1, (c) x = 0.25, and (d) x = 0.75. The negative slope observed for GdVO4 at low temperature suggests a first-order-like character of the spin-flop-like field-induced anomaly.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This work investigates $	ext{Gd}_{1-x}	ext{Er}_x	ext{VO}_4$ to engineer materials for cryogenic refrigeration by tuning their magnetic properties. By substituting Gd with Er, the researchers systematically suppressed the magnetic ordering temperature and modified field-induced anomalies. The key finding is that weak Er doping optimizes the balance of competing magnetic interactions, leading to superior magnetocaloric performance.

**Why it may be interesting.** The study provides a clear experimental demonstration of how chemical pressure (lattice contraction) can be used as a tuning knob in magnetic materials to optimize thermodynamic responses like MCE, which is relevant for understanding phase transitions and energy storage in condensed matter systems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study aims to develop efficient solid-state refrigeration strategies by tuning magnetic ordering and magnetocaloric performance in rare-earth oxides.

**Main result.** Weak Er substitution effectively tunes the competition among magnetic interactions, optimizing the balance between magnetic ordering and available spin entropy for enhanced cryogenic cooling.

**Method.** The research combines structural characterization (XRD) with comprehensive magnetic measurements, including temperature-dependent magnetization and isothermal magnetocaloric effect calculations.

**Model / system.** The physical system is polycrystalline zircon-type rare-earth vanadates, specifically $	ext{Gd}_{1-x}	ext{Er}_x	ext{VO}_4$, where chemical substitution ($x$) acts as the tuning parameter.

**Key observables.** Magnetic ordering temperature ($	ext{T}_{	ext{N}}$), maximum magnetic entropy change ($\Delta S_{	ext{mag}}$), lattice parameters ($a, c$), and relative cooling power (RCP).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The doping concentration $x$ of $	ext{Er}^{3+}$ on $	ext{Gd}^{3+}$, applied magnetic field ($\mu_0\Delta H = 7 	ext{ T}$), and cryogenic temperatures.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The primary assumption is that tuning the rare-earth ion size via substitution can systematically control the competing magnetic interactions (exchange, dipolar coupling) to optimize MCE.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate XRD patterns showing lattice contraction with increasing Er content, and magnetization curves demonstrating the suppression of the spin-flop anomaly and enhancement of $\Delta S_{	ext{mag}}$ at low $x$.

**Paper structure.** The paper follows a logical progression: structural confirmation via XRD; investigation of magnetic ordering temperature suppression ($	ext{T}_{	ext{N}}$) and field anomalies; finally, quantitative measurement and optimization of the magnetocaloric effect ($\Delta S_{	ext{mag}}$) to identify optimal compositions.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Chemical substitution offers an effective route to tune magnetic ordering and magnetocaloric performance in rare-earth oxides for cryogenic refrigeration. Here we investigate the structural evo lution, magnetic properties, and magnetocaloric effect of polycrystalline zircon-type Gd1-xErxVO4 (x=0, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, and 0.75). Powder X-ray diffraction confirms that all samples crystallize in the tetragonal zircon structure without detectable impurity phases. Substitution of Gd3+ by the smaller Er3+ ion produces a systematic lattice contraction and modifies the magnetic behavior of the rare-earth sublattice. In particular, the magnetic ordering temperature is suppressed from 3.65(2) K in GdVO4 to 2.76(2) K in Gd0.9Er0.1VO4 , accompanied by a weakening of the spin-flop-like field-induced anomaly observed in the parent compound. A low Er concentration correspondingly improves the low-temperature magnetocaloric performance, with Gd0.9Er0.1VO4 exhibiting a max imum magnetic entropy change of 45.1 J kg-1 K-1 for mu_0 Delta H=7T. These results demonstrate that weak Er substitution effectively tunes the competition among exchange interactions, dipolar coupling, and magnetic anisotropy, optimizing the balance between magnetic ordering and available spin entropy in zircon-type rare-earth vanadates, which is crucial for developing efficient cryogenic refrigeration materials.

</details>

### [Entanglement-assisted continuous-variable concatenated codes for encoding qubits or oscillators](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09379v1)

**Authors:** Nihar Ranjan Dash, Sanjoy Dutta, R. Srikanth, Subhashish Banerjee  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.09379v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `entanglement & information structure` **1/5** · `quantum measurements` **1/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2606.09379_figures/2606.09379_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 3: Encoding circuit for [[3, 1, 3; 2]]2 entanglement- assisted repetition code.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2606.09379_figures/2606.09379_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 2: (Color online) code structure of an C[[3,1,3;2]] ▷CGKP code with outer [[3, 1, 3; 2]] EA-repetition code and inner GKP code: A single logical qubit of the outer [[3, 1, 3; 2]] EA-repetition code represented as a white circle encoded into three physical qubits (blue circles) by utilizing two pre-shared entangled bits( green circles). The inner GKP code further encodes these three qubits, along with the two entangled bits, into harmonic oscillators, represented by yellow and red cir- cles, respectively.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2606.09379_figures/2606.09379_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 5: (Color online) Logical failure probability of C[[3,1,3;2]] ▷CGKP, C[[5,1,3]] ▷CGKP, and CGKP ▷C[[3,1,3;2]] codes as a function of the standard deviation σ, depicted as the blue, green, and blue plots, respectively.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper develops novel entanglement-assisted continuous-variable concatenated codes by combining EA stabilizer codes with GKP bosonic codes. It theoretically demonstrates that these concatenated structures effectively suppress quadrature errors, achieving a variance reduction proportional to $1/n$. This advances the field of fault-tolerant quantum computation for bosonic systems.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant as it pushes the boundaries of quantum information theory by merging two advanced QEC paradigms (EA and CV bosonic codes). The focus on error suppression factors provides concrete theoretical benchmarks for future experimental implementations in quantum optics or superconducting circuits.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The research aims to enhance quantum error correction rates by combining entanglement-assisted (EA) stabilizer codes with bosonic continuous-variable (CV) codes, specifically the Gottesman-Kitaev-Preskill (GKP) code.

**Main result.** They propose and analyze two concatenated structures ($	ext{EA} 	riangleright 	ext{GKP}$ and $	ext{GKP} 	riangleright 	ext{EA}$) that demonstrate significant suppression of quadrature errors, achieving a variance reduction factor of $1/n$ in the general case.

**Method.** The analysis involves deriving logical state representations for concatenated codes, calculating syndrome measurements from Gaussian unitary encoders, and employing Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) to estimate and correct residual quadrature errors.

**Model / system.** The system models quantum information encoded in bosonic modes using GKP states. The theoretical framework utilizes stabilizer formalism generalized to include entanglement assistance via ebits for error correction.

**Key observables.** Logical failure probability ($p_L$), residual quadrature error variances ($	ext{Var}(\zeta^*_q) = 	ext{Var}(\zeta^*_p)$), and the success probability of bit identification ($p_{	ext{succ}}$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The number of qubits/modes ($n$), the noise variance ($\sigma^2$), and the entanglement resource (ebits).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes independent and identically distributed (iid) Gaussian random shift errors, and for the general result, it relies on syndrome measurements falling within a specific range $[-\sqrt{\pi}/2, \sqrt{\pi}/2]$.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 illustrates encoding circuits for GKP Bell pairs using both computational and canonical bases. Figure 5 compares the logical failure probability versus standard deviation for different concatenation orders.

**Paper structure.** The paper first establishes the theoretical basis by combining EA concepts with bosonic codes (GKP). It then details two specific concatenation orders ($	ext{EA} 	riangleright 	ext{GKP}$ and $	ext{GKP} 	riangleright 	ext{EA}$) using examples like $n=3$. The core analysis involves deriving error suppression factors, culminating in a general theorem showing $1/n$ variance reduction for the $	ext{CGKP} 	riangleright C[[n,1,n;n-1]]$ code.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Entanglement-assisted (EA) stabilizer codes enhance the rate of error correction in relation to codes with no pre-shared entanglement. Meanwhile, bosonic error-correcting codes, such as the Gottesman-Kitaev-Preskill (GKP) code, can be concatenated with qubit stabilizer codes to significantly reduce the logical failure probability of those stabilizer codes. First, we combine the above two concepts to propose an EA version of the qubit-into-oscillators concatenated code that chains an EA-stabilizer (outer) code with a GKP (inner) code. As an example we present a three-qubit EA-repetition concatenated with a GKP code. Second, we propose an EA version of the non-Gaussian oscillator-into-oscillators concatenated code that chains a GKP (outer) code with an EA-stabilizer (inner) code. As an example we present a GKP code concatenated with a three-qubit EA repetition code that uses two maximally entangled modes (emodes) and suppresses the variances of both position and momentum quadrature errors of a data mode. Furthermore, we generalize the latter example to a family of GKP code concatenated with a $n$-qubit EA repetition code that uses ${n-1}$ emodes and suppresses the variances of both position and momentum quadrature errors of a data mode by a factor ${1/n}$.

</details>

### [Exploring the landscape of compact magic-state distillation factories](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07734v1)

**Authors:** Hugo Jacinto, Xavier Valcarce, Victor Barizien, Élie Gouzien, Nicolas Sangouard  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.07734v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `entanglement & information structure` **1/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **1/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2606.07734_figures/2606.07734_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1. Summary of the exploration of compact magic-state distillation protocols. Each dot represents a distillation protocol implemented on N logical qubits that consumes n noisy magic states and outputs a single magic state with output error scaling as pout ∝pd in, where d is the distillation distance. Previously known protocols include the 15-to-1 protocol of Ref. [22], referred to as [Bravyi2005] on the figure, implemented on N = 5 qubits, and the 49-to-1 protocol of Ref. [27], [Bravyi2012], implemented on N = 14 qubits. Ref. [38], referred to as [Nezami2022] on the figure, proved a no-go result showing that any protocol with n + k ≤38 satisfies d ≤3. In this work, we introduce several...</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2606.07734_figures/2606.07734_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 2. Gate teleportation and Clifford correction circuit to perform a Pπ/8 rotation [16]. First a |T⟩state is injected and the entire system is jointly measured in P ⊗Z. A first Pauli correction consisting of a Pπ/4 gate is applied to the N qubits if the outcome of the measurement is −1. The injected qubit undergoes a Hadamard operation before being measured in the Z basis. Another Clifford correction, namely Pπ/2, is fi- nally performed on the first N qubits.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This work tackles the critical problem of generating high-fidelity magic states necessary for fault-tolerant quantum computing. By reformulating distillation protocols using classical error-correcting codes, the authors used SAT solvers to derive new theoretical bounds and construct highly compact distillation factories. This advances the understanding of resource overhead by minimizing both qubit count and required operations.

**Why it may be interesting.** While focused on quantum computation theory, the rigorous application of classical coding theory and computational search techniques (SAT solving) provides powerful generalizable methods for analyzing resource overhead in complex physical systems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The primary challenge is developing efficient, fault-tolerant methods for producing high-fidelity magic states (like $|Tangle$) required for universal quantum computation.

**Main result.** The authors derived new, compact distillation protocols achieving higher distances ($d=5$ for T-to-T) using fewer physical qubits than previously known. They also established no-go theorems relating protocol parameters.

**Method.** They employed a combination of classical error-correcting code theory and Boolean Satisfiability (SAT) solvers to systematically constrain the design space of distillation protocols.

**Model / system.** The focus is on implementing logical T gates using magic state distillation factories based on the repetition code. The analysis involves mapping quantum constraints onto algebraic structures solvable by SAT solvers.

**Key observables.** Distillation distance ($d$), number of physical qubits ($N$), and the required T-count ($n$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The relationship between $N$, $d$, and $n$ is central; specific results include achieving $d=5$ on $N=11$ qubits.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Protocols are analyzed by framing the problem using classical error-correcting code structures, simplifying the initial quantum description via Pauli product rotations.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the comparison of T-count vs. number of logical qubits for various distillation distances and show circuit diagrams for gate teleportation/correction.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from general necessity (magic state distillation) to specific mathematical tools (classical codes, SAT solvers), deriving bounds and constructing optimized protocols for both $|Tangle$ and $\sqrt{T}$ states.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Producing high-fidelity magic states using the smallest possible amount of physical qubits and operations stands as a very important challenge to achieve fault-tolerant quantum computation at scale. Besides emerging proposals for alternative methods such as cultivation, magic state distillation remains essential for achieving very low error rates. Known distillation protocols are usually built through quantum codes derived from triorthogonal matrices. Here, exploiting the specific noise structure present in magic state distillation protocols, we show that classical error-correcting codes offer a simpler framework for deriving these protocols. This formulation is particularly well suited to systematic numerical and analytical studies of distillation protocols involving a fixed number of qubits. Specifically, we use a SAT solver to derive a series of no-go theorems that relate key figures of merit, including the number of qubits, the protocol depth, the factory distance, and the prefactor in the output error rate. For instance, we prove that any $T$-to-$T$ state distillation protocol using fewer than eight qubits can detect at most three errors, while any $T$-to-$\mathrm{CC}Z$ state distillation protocol using fewer than eight qubits can detect at most two errors. Our results also include new distillation protocols with the smallest number of qubits for a given distance in the literature, namely distance 4 and 5 $T$-to-$T$ state protocols supported on 10 and 11 qubits, as well as distance 3 and 4 $T$-to-$\mathrm{CC}Z$ state distillation protocols supported on 9 and 10 qubits.

</details>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper investigates whether the confidence score generated by a Graph Neural Network (GNN) can replace the traditional 'logical gap' used for decoding measurements in quantum error correction. By comparing performance on the rotated surface code, the authors demonstrate that the GNN-derived soft information leads to lower logical error rates and better calibration than the standard Minimum Weight Perfect Matching approach. This suggests GNNs are a powerful tool for extracting reliable confidence metrics from syndrome data.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a concrete example of applying machine learning techniques (GNNs) to extract physically meaningful soft information in quantum error correction, offering a scalable alternative when computationally intensive methods like MWPM are impractical.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** To determine if a Graph Neural Network (GNN) decoder's raw output confidence score can serve as a reliable, learned proxy for the established 'logical gap' used in quantum error correction decoding.

**Main result.** Post-selection based on the GNN logit yields a lower logical error rate than using the conventional Minimum Weight Perfect Matching (MWPM) complementary gap across tested noise regimes. The GNN also shows better calibration towards ideal theoretical estimates.

**Method.** The study compares two soft information measures—the GNN's output logit and the MWPM gap—by testing their efficacy in post-selecting measurements from a quantum error-correcting code.

**Model / system.** The analysis is performed on the rotated surface code using syndrome measurements generated under uniform circuit-level noise. The comparison involves decoding performance across varying acceptance rates ($\kappa$) and physical error probabilities ($p$).

**Key observables.** Logical Error Rate (LER), signed confidence distribution, and calibration slope ($\alpha$) relative to the ideal posterior log-likelihood ratio.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Physical error rate ($p=0.005$), code distance ($d$), number of stabilizer rounds ($r$), and acceptance rate ($\kappa$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The primary assumption is that ranking measurements by confidence score (rather than absolute scale) determines the post-selection advantage, and that the GNN can learn sufficient soft information without explicit knowledge of matching probabilities.

**Figures summary.** Figures compare LER vs. acceptance rate for both decoders; other figures show probability distributions and empirical calibrations of the signed confidence scores against theoretical expectations.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem by comparing GNN decoding to MWPM gap estimation, presents quantitative results showing superior performance (lower LER) for the GNN, analyzes the shape and calibration of the confidence scores, and concludes with implications for general decoder design.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

To utilize quantum error-correcting codes, a decoder must infer the logical sector from the measured syndrome. Beyond producing a hard logical decision, some decoders provide soft information that estimates the reliability of that decision. For minimum-weight perfect matching (MWPM), a common confidence measure is the complementary, or logical, gap. Here we test whether the logit of a graph neural network (GNN) decoder can act as a learned proxy for the logical gap. Using a pretrained GNN for the rotated surface code under uniform circuit-level noise [Physical Review Research, 7(2):023181, 2025], we compare its soft output with the MWPM complementary gap on the same sampled syndromes. We find that post-selection based on the GNN logit yields a lower logical error rate than one based on the MWPM gap. Shot-by-shot, the signed GNN confidence distribution resembles the signed MWPM gap at low and intermediate values, but assigns higher confidence to many correctly decoded shots. While both scores approximate the posterior log-likelihood ratio, the GNN confidence magnitude is closer to its ideal value. These results show that a neural-network decoder trained only on syndromes and logical labels learns both gap-like discrimination and a quantitative confidence scale, enabling confidence-based post-selection when MWPM gap estimates are unavailable, costly, or poorly matched to the noise model.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper analyzes methods for achieving transatlantic quantum entanglement distribution using satellites and stratospheric relays. By modeling various link architectures, the authors determined that a hybrid LEO satellite and HAP system provides the highest secure key rate ($\approx 5 	imes 10^6$ bits/year). This demonstrates the significant advantage of incorporating low-cost, maneuverable atmospheric relays over single high-altitude satellites.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work directly addresses the engineering challenges of scaling quantum communication networks over continental distances by proposing a practical hybrid space/air relay solution that significantly outperforms purely satellite-based approaches.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study investigates feasible architectures for distributing quantum entanglement across transatlantic distances (6,500 km) to establish a global quantum network.

**Main result.** A hybrid architecture combining an entangled photon source on a Low Earth Orbit (LEO) satellite with two passive High-Altitude Platform (HAP) relays yields the highest entanglement distribution rate ($\approx 5 	imes 10^6$ secure key bits/year).

**Method.** The authors modeled various free-space link configurations, comparing single MEO satellites against hybrid LEO+HAP systems by analyzing link budgets and achievable key rates.

**Model / system.** The system involves ground stations communicating with space assets (LEO/MEO satellites) and stratospheric HAPs acting as passive optical relays. The analysis incorporates orbital mechanics, free-space link loss calculations, and quantum key distribution metrics.

**Key observables.** Entanglement distribution rate (secure key bits per year), Quantum Bit Error Rate (QBER), and violation of the CHSH Bell inequality parameter ($S$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Transatlantic distance ($\sim 6,500$ km); LEO altitude ($\sim 1,200$ km); HAP altitude ($\sim 20$ km); Ground receiver aperture sizes (e.g., $30 	ext{ cm}$ vs $1 	ext{ m}$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study assumes that HAPs act purely as passive relays and does not rely on quantum memories or repeaters for entanglement distribution.

**Figures summary.** Figures compare link loss and elevation angle variations across different satellite constellations (MEO vs. LEO+HAP); Figure 7 illustrates the relationship between Bell-violation parameters and QBER requirements.

**Paper structure.** The paper systematically compares multiple link architectures (single MEO, multi-LEO, LEO+HAP) by calculating key performance indicators like achievable secure key rates and verifying entanglement preservation via Bell tests.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

To explore the pathways toward establishing a global quantum network, we investigate several link architectures for transatlantic quantum entanglement distribution over a 6,500 km ground distance. We define free-space link configurations involving satellites and stratospheric high altitude platforms (HAPs), using today's technology and without relying on quantum memories and repeaters. Considering link budgets, space radiation, orbital characteristics, and system complexity we find that a hybrid architecture consisting of an entangled photon source located on a low Earth orbit (LEO) satellite supported by two passive optical relays located on HAPs provides the overall highest entanglement distribution rate. In addition, the satellite HAP architecture offers practical advantages in payload design and launch requirements, and the ability to lower the weather-related link interruptions assuming some maneuverability of HAPs. Overall, this hybrid configuration yields on the order of 5X10^6 secure key bits per year using 30 cm aperture ground receivers, nearly two orders of magnitude higher than achievable with a single MEO satellite and 1 m aperture ground receivers. Our results highlight the major benefits of hybrid satellite HAP architectures by reducing system complexity while enabling scalable and more accessible long-range quantum communication networks.

</details>

**Summary.** This work develops a quantum interactive proof system (qIOP) to characterize problems in the complexity class QMA. By combining quantum locally testable codes with classical PCP techniques, the authors show that QMA can be verified using only polylogarithmic measurements from the verifier. This result is significant as it provides an information-theoretic characterization of QMA without assuming a quantum PCP theorem.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides deep connections between complexity theory (QMA) and quantum coding theory, offering tools for verifying complex quantum computations efficiently.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper aims to establish a quantum analogue of interactive oracle proofs (qIOP) to characterize Quantum Merlin-Arthur (QMA) problems.

**Main result.** They prove that every language in QMA possesses an efficient qIOP where the verifier only needs to read a polylogarithmic number of qubits, providing a local and robust characterization of QMA.

**Method.** The protocol combines quantum Locally Testable Codes (qLTCs) with classical techniques like Probabilistically Checkable Proofs of Proximity (PCPP). The verification process involves multiple stages: measurement extraction and constraint checking.

**Model / system.** The model is the qIOP, involving a quantum verifier interacting with an unbounded prover. It utilizes quantum error-correcting codes (qLTCs) to encode states, which are tested via local measurements on small subsets of qubits.

**Key observables.** Acceptance probability of the energy test ($	ext{Pr}[	ext{energy test accepts}]$), fidelity between state evolution operators, and measurement outcomes in Z or X bases.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The verifier reads a polylogarithmic number of qubits ($q$), while total communication is polynomial ($\ell$). The success relies on the code distance ($d_q$) and locality ($q$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The result holds in the absence of a quantum PCP theorem, relying on the existence of suitable qLTC codes with specific parameters.

**Paper structure.** The paper builds from defining the qIOP model to implementing measurement extraction using qLTCs (Single/Global Measurement Extraction), followed by a final energy test phase that combines these results to prove the QMA characterization.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The model of interactive oracle proofs (IOP) generalizes the notion of probabilistically checkable proof (PCP), in which a static proof is verified probabilistically by querying a small number of bits, to the interactive setting: a polynomial-time verifier interacts with an unbounded prover, but is restricted to only reading a small number of bits, in total, from the messages sent by the prover. IOPs provide a relaxed setting in which to study local probabilistic verification. They have proved instrumental in devising efficient methods for verification through subsequent compilation into non-interactive or succinct protocols.   We study a quantum analogue of interactive oracle proofs (qIOP) in which the verifier and communication are both allowed to be quantum; yet the verifier is restricted to perform measurements only on a small number of qubits received from the prover. Our main result is a qIOP for any language in QMA, in which the total communication is polynomial but the verifier only reads a polylogarithmic number of qubits in total. The protocol has completeness parameter exponentially close to $1$ and soundness bounded away from $1$ by a constant. In the absence of a quantum PCP theorem, this provides the first information-theoretically sound local and robust characterization of QMA, albeit interactive.   Our protocol combines the use of a quantum locally testable code (LTC) with classical techniques, notably probabilistically checkable proofs of proximity (PCPP). We avoid the necessity for complex multi-qubit tests employed in other settings by leveraging the local indistinguishability property of the quantum LTC.

</details>

**Authors:** Yuhang Deng, Gabriel Mee, Tyler Wannamaker, Keke Feng, Mingyu Xu, Weiwei Xie, M. Brian Maple

**Summary.** This work experimentally maps the superconducting phase diagram of UTe2 under high pressure, revealing a striking dichotomy between its orthorhombic and tetragonal phases. The findings suggest that structural changes tune the pairing mechanism from an unconventional state to a more conventional one in the tetragonal phase. This highlights how external tuning can stabilize different types of correlated electronic ground states.

**Why it may be interesting.** While focused on condensed matter, the comparison of BCS-like vs. unconventional pairing symmetries under external tuning (pressure) provides a model for understanding how quantum critical points can stabilize different types of correlated ground states.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study aims to map out the superconducting transition temperature ($T_c$) versus pressure ($P$) phase diagram of UTe2, specifically investigating the structural and electronic dichotomy between its orthorhombic and high-pressure tetragonal phases.

**Main result.** Superconductivity in the tetragonal phase is robust and appears more conventional (orbitally limited) compared to the fragile, unconventional superconductivity seen in the orthorhombic phase. The normal state exhibits a persistent 'knee' feature ($T^{**}$) linked to potential magnetic ordering.

**Method.** The research relies on electrical transport measurements ($	ext{R}(T)$) and AC magnetic susceptibility ($\chi(T,P)$) performed under high pressure using Diamond Anvil Cells (DACs).

**Model / system.** The system is Uranium ditelluride ($	ext{UTe}_2$), a heavy-fermion superconductor known for complex interplay between structural transitions, magnetism, and superconductivity. The study compares the superconducting behavior across two distinct crystal structures.

**Key observables.** Superconducting transition temperature ($T_c$), upper critical field ($	ext{H}_{c2}$), normal-state resistivity $ho(T)$ exhibiting a 'knee' feature ($T^{**}$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Pressure range up to 16 GPa; characteristic fields like the Pauli paramagnetic limit and orbital limits.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes that structural changes (orthorhombic $\leftrightarrow$ tetragonal) fundamentally alter the pairing mechanism, leading to different superconducting symmetries.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate representative resistance vs. temperature curves showing SC transitions in the tetragonal phase at various pressures, and potentially show the $T-P$ phase diagram.

**Paper structure.** The paper systematically measures transport properties across structural transitions, contrasting the behavior of superconductivity (SC) and normal state features ($T^{**}$) between the two phases to deduce underlying electronic mechanisms.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Electrical transport and magnetic measurements have been made on UTe2 under pressure P up to approximately 16 GPa to determine the superconducting transition temperature Tc vs P phase diagram in the high-pressure tetragonal phase. Superconductivity emerges near 5 GPa, coincident with the orthorhombic to tetragonal phase transition; in the tetragonal phase, Tc reaches a maximum value of approximately 4 K at 6 GPa and then decreases with P and appears to vanish near 18 GPa. Tetragonal UTe2 has a relatively small upper critical field Hc2(0) $\approx$ 1.2 T at 5.3 GPa, smaller than the Pauli paramagnetic limit, and is orbitally limited with a coherence length $ξ$tetra $\approx$ 16.5 nm. This small value of Hc2(0) favors more conventional superconductivity; in contrast, the large values of Hc2(T) for orthorhombic UTe2 exceed the Pauli paramagnetic limit in all three crystallographic directions and have been attributed to unconventional superconductivity, widely believed to involve spin-triplet pairing. The temperature-pressure phase diagram of UTe2 shows a striking dichotomy: a narrow, fragile, unconventional superconducting region in the orthorhombic phase vs a broad, robust, and more conventional superconducting dome in the tetragonal phase. This dichotomy is consistent with the proposal that U-dimers, present (absent) in the orthorhombic (tetragonal) phase, may play a role in spin-triplet superconductivity of orthorhombic UTe2. In the tetragonal phase, the normal-state electrical resistivity $ρ$(T) exhibits metallic behavior with a knee between 200 and 240 K that depends weakly on P and most likely marks the onset of a transition to a magnetically ordered phase that coexists with superconductivity.

</details>

**Summary.** The paper introduces an advanced forward gradient estimator framework to overcome the prohibitive measurement cost associated with calculating gradients for large parameterised quantum circuits. By adaptively optimizing both the number of random directions and the shots allocated per direction, the proposed QUIVER optimizer significantly outperforms established methods across various quantum machine learning benchmarks.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a concrete, resource-aware algorithmic improvement for variational quantum algorithms, directly addressing the primary computational bottleneck in scaling up near-term quantum hardware applications.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Training parameterised quantum circuits (PQCs) is bottlenecked by the measurement cost of gradient estimation, which scales poorly with the number of trainable parameters.

**Main result.** A forward gradient estimator framework, combined with an adaptive optimizer (QUIVER), achieves orders of magnitude more efficient training than standard methods like the Parameter-Shift Rule for large quantum neural networks.

**Method.** The authors propose a forward gradient estimator based on the forward mode of automatic differentiation, averaging random directional derivatives to reconstruct the full gradient efficiently. They combine this with an adaptive optimizer that jointly optimizes the number of directions and measurement shots.

**Model / system.** The work focuses on training PQCs used in Variational Quantum Algorithms (VQA) like VQE and QNNs. Benchmarks include classifying ECG5000 data, MNIST images, solving QAOA MaxCut problems, and estimating ground states for TFIM Hamiltonians.

**Key observables.** Energy error ($|E - E_0|$), approximation ratio ($r$), Mean Squared Error (MSE) of the gradient estimator, and total measurement shot count.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Number of parameters ($N$), number of random directions sampled ($V$), shots per direction ($M$), step size ($\epsilon$), and learning rate ($\eta$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on assumptions regarding the convergence properties (e.g., PL condition locally) and uses central finite-difference approximations for directional derivatives, which introduce bias.

**Figures summary.** Figures compare the measurement shot costs of various gradient estimators (PS vs. Forward) showing significant savings with forward gradients; other figures show performance comparisons across multiple benchmark tasks (QAOA, VQE, MNIST) demonstrating superior convergence rates for QUIVER.

**Paper structure.** The paper systematically reviews existing gradient estimation techniques (PS, SPSA, RCD), introduces the unified forward gradient estimator parameterized by $V$, develops the adaptive optimization strategy (QUIVER) via joint $(V, M)$ minimization, and validates this approach across multiple complex quantum machine learning benchmarks.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Training parameterised quantum circuits (PQCs) on quantum hardware is bottlenecked by the measurement cost of gradient estimation, which under the parameter-shift rule scales linearly in the number of trainable parameters and dominates the total shot budget of training at scale. In this work, we propose a framework of forward gradient estimators for PQCs, based on the forward mode of automatic differentiation, that yields an unbiased estimator of the gradient by averaging a freely tunable number of random directional derivatives and recovers SPSA, random coordinate descent, and the parameter-shift rule as limiting cases, with no ancilla qubits or controlled-gate overhead. We prove that stochastic quantum forward gradient descent converges under standard assumptions, with an explicit second-moment expansion that interpolates between the single-direction extreme of SPSA and the full-gradient extreme of parameter-shift. Within this framework we derive QUIVER (Quantum Iterative V-adaptive Estimator Rule), an adaptive optimiser for parameterised circuits whose update rule follows from a closed-form minimum measurement-cost allocation. We show numerically that forward gradients train Hamming-weight-preserving orthogonal quantum neural networks with up to 60 qubits and 1770 parameters on the ECG5000 and MNIST datasets orders of magnitude more efficiently than the parameter-shift rule. We also demonstrate that our proposed QUIVER optimiser can outperform iCANS and gCANS measurement-frugal optimisers on optimisation problems using the quantum approximate optimisation algorithm and quantum simulation with the variational quantum eigensolver.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper explores alternative geometric foundations for quantum gravity by focusing on teleparallel gravity, which replaces curvature with torsion as the fundamental field strength. It argues that using coframe and spin-connection variables provides a natural gauge structure suitable for quantization. The work aims to identify necessary conceptual ingredients rather than providing a complete solution to quantizing spacetime itself.

**Why it may be interesting.** While focused on gravity, the development of a constrained quantum field theory based on gauge potentials (coframe/spin-connection) shares conceptual parallels with advanced topics in quantum information theory involving gauge symmetries and constraints.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The fundamental incompatibility between Quantum Field Theory (QFT) and General Relativity (GR) at the Planck scale, necessitating a new framework for quantizing gravity.

**Main result.** The paper proposes that reformulating gravity using coframe and spin-connection variables, where dynamics are governed by torsion rather than curvature, provides a geometrically refined starting point for quantum gravity research.

**Method.** It compares various QG approaches (LQG, String Theory, Asymptotic Safety) and develops the teleparallel framework by treating the coframe/spin-connection pair as independent gauge variables.

**Model / system.** The model is a theoretical extension of General Relativity into the teleparallel gravity sector, utilizing the coframe ($\mathbf{h}^a_\mu$) and spin-connection ($\omega^a_{b\mu}$) to encode gravitational dynamics in torsion. It analyzes matter coupling (Klein-Gordon, Dirac) within this framework.

**Key observables.** Torsion ($T$), Curvature ($R$), Nonmetricity ($Q$), and the constraint algebra generators ($\hat{H}, \hat{D}_i, \hat{J}^{ab}$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Planck scale; coupling constants $\kappa$; mass $m$ (for Proca field).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The primary assumption is that gravity can be reformulated using the coframe/spin-connection pair to achieve a gauge-like structure, bypassing some limitations of metric formulations.

**Figures summary.** Not specified in the notes; the text references comparative tables (TABLE I) summarizing different QG approaches based on their fundamental variables.

**Paper structure.** The paper first critiques QFTCS and reviews major QG candidates. It then introduces the teleparallel framework, detailing its geometric structure using coframe/spin-connection variables, deriving actions like $F(T)$, and finally outlining the canonical quantization program using constraint operators.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We revisit quantum field theory in curved spacetime (QFTCS) as a semi-classical framework for quantum matter on classical geometries, emphasizing its limitations, including vacuum ambiguity and background dependence. We briefly review major approaches to quantum gravity (QG), including Loop Quantum Gravity (LQG), string theory, and asymptotic safety, highlighting their conceptual challenges. Motivated by these issues, we outline a teleparallel framework based on coframe and spin-connection variables, where gravity is encoded in torsion rather than curvature. This framework naturally incorporates local Lorentz symmetry and fermionic couplings while displaying a gauge-like structure. We argue that the coframe/spin-connection pair provides an alternative and geometrically refined description of gravitational variables, which may serve as a useful starting point for future investigations of QG. The purpose of this work is not to provide a complete quantization of teleparallel gravity but to identify the geometric and conceptual ingredients that such a formulation would require.

</details>

Only motions along the 𝑋𝑋-axis are described. 𝑥𝑥 and 𝛿𝛿𝛿𝛿± are displacements of the central atom and two nnn atoms, respectively. (B) Potential energy, 𝑢𝑢, as a function of 𝑥𝑥.
