**Summary.** This work develops a theoretical framework to explain excess specific heat observed below the Debye prediction in perfect crystals. It attributes this anomaly to energy fluctuations caused by anharmonic interactions involving next-nearest-neighbor atoms modulating harmonic lattice vibrations. The theory successfully models these deviations using molecular dynamics simulations and provides insights applicable to amorphous materials.

**Why it may be interesting.** The connection drawn between lattice dynamics, anharmonicity, and anomalous low-temperature thermodynamics is highly relevant for understanding energy transport in complex quantum systems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the discrepancy between measured specific heat at low temperatures and the predictions of Debye's $T^3$-law in high-purity crystals.

**Main result.** Excess energy fluctuations, arising from anharmonic interactions involving next-nearest-neighbor atoms, modify the standard relation between energy fluctuations and specific heat, providing a quantitative explanation for the observed deviations.

**Method.** The authors develop a theory based on time- and phase-averaging of molecular dynamics simulations, treating localized excitations as decoupled from the main heat bath.

**Model / system.** The system modeled is crystalline solids (e.g., $	ext{SiO}_2$) analyzed via Molecular Dynamics (MD) simulations using the Lennard-Jones potential. The theory focuses on how anharmonic interactions between next-nearest-neighbor atoms modulate harmonic nearest-neighbor modes.

**Key observables.** Specific heat ($C_V$), energy fluctuation ratios ($\langle(\Delta t u)^2angle / \langle(\Delta t k)^2angle$), and correlation functions ($\Gamma_{0,0}(	au)$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Low temperatures ($T \ll 300$ K), the ratio of potential to kinetic energy fluctuations, and the anharmonic coupling strength derived from nnn interactions.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The theory assumes that localized excitations are decoupled from the heat bath (fast modulations) and uses semiclassical approximations for the energy modulation.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate time-resolved specific heat measurements showing deviations from Debye's law, and MD results confirming the divergence of fluctuation ratios at low T due to anharmonic terms.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses by first presenting experimental evidence of $C_V$ deviation (Chunk 1), then developing a theoretical framework using MD simulations on L-J models to quantify excess energy fluctuations (Chunk 2), and finally generalizing this theory to predict the functional form of the specific heat anomaly ($T^{\lambda+1}$) based on anharmonic coupling (Chunk 3).

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Measured specific heats often exceed Debye's T^3-law, even in high-purity single crystals. Analogous excess energy fluctuations in molecular dynamics (MD) simulations of crystals with no defects come from fast energy modulations involving next-nearest-neighbor atoms. Here, a theory is developed for these modulations, based on time- and phase-averaging followed by thermal averaging. This order of averaging is guided by evidence from the simulations and various experimental techniques showing that localized excitations are decoupled from the heat bath. Emergent nonextensivity is interpreted by analogy with anomalous diffusion. The theory modifies the standard relation between energy fluctuations and specific heat, giving good agreement with the simulations and new insight into many measurements. The theory may also provide a basis for understanding excess specific heat in amorphous materials and anomalous noise in quantum devices.

</details>

**Authors:** Francesco Cioni, Lorenzo Cavicchi, Nazzareno Africani, Giacomo Mazza, Fabio Taddei, Amir Yacoby, Marco Polini  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.09812v1>

<sub>FIG. 1. Topological phase diagram of the skyrmion Chern- band continuum model [18, 19]—panel (a)—as a function of the two parameters V and ψ in Eq. (6). White regions in- dicate topologically trivial phases while colored regions de- note phases with C ̸= 0. The triangle corresponds to twisted MoTe2. Notice the chirality reversal in passing from posi- tive (black triangle) to negative...</sub>

**Summary.** This work investigates twisted TMD homobilayers exhibiting Chern insulating states by analyzing the ground-state current density. The authors demonstrate that the maximum local current amplitude serves as a robust, measurable marker for non-zero topological order ($C 
eq 0$). By calculating both the currents and the resulting magnetic fields, they provide theoretical evidence linking bulk topology to observable real-space electromagnetic signatures.

**Why it may be interesting.** The direct link established between a measurable local current density (a real-space observable) and a fundamental topological invariant ($C$) provides an experimental roadmap for detecting exotic quantum states in solid-state materials, which is highly relevant to condensed matter theory.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** To determine if a measurable local quantity exists that acts as a real-space marker for topological phases with non-zero Chern number in twisted TMD homobilayers.

**Main result.** The maximum amplitude of the ground-state current density in the bulk, $\max |J(r)|$, is shown to be a reliable local proxy for detecting non-trivial Chern insulating states ($C 
eq 0$).

**Method.** Numerical calculations are performed on a continuum model Hamiltonian describing twisted TMD homobilayers, analyzing the ground-state current density ($\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{r})$) and its associated magnetic field using the Biot-Savart law.

**Model / system.** The system is modeled by twisted transition metal dichalcogenide (TMD) homobilayers hosting Chern insulating states. The physics is captured by a continuum model Hamiltonian involving intra-layer and inter-layer potentials, which breaks time-reversal symmetry.

**Key observables.** Local current density $\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{r})$ (exhibiting whirlpools), the maximum bulk current amplitude $\max |J(r)|$, Hall conductance ($\sigma_{xy}$), and the generated magnetic field components ($B_x, B_y, B_z$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The topological invariant is the Chern number ($C$); key parameters include the twist angle ($\psi$) and ribbon width ($W$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on a continuum model description for twisted TMD homobilayers and assumes that local current density accurately tracks global topology.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the Topological Phase Diagram showing $C 
eq 0$ regions correlating with bulk current whirlpools. Other figures show $\sigma_{xy}$ scaling with width ($W$) and magnetic field components generated by the currents at various distances ($d$).

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from establishing the local current marker ($\max |J(r)|$) in the bulk, to analyzing the contributions of intra-band vs. inter-band currents, and finally examining how global quantities like $\sigma_{xy}$ scale with finite width $W$ and how the resulting magnetic field can be measured.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Recent materials advances have made it possible to fabricate twisted transition metal dichalcogenide homobilayers. These systems have been shown to host integer and fractional Chern insulating states. Because of spontaneous time reversal symmetry breaking, their ground state harbors intriguing spin-polarized currents with whirlpools on the moiré length scale that can be measured by scanning probe methods. We first provide a quantitative analysis of these persistent currents and then show that the maximum of the amplitude of the current density in the bulk of the sample is an accurate tracker of topological order. We conclude by calculating how the quantization of the Hall conductance is affected by finite-size effects.

</details>

**Authors:** Andrew J. Baldwin, Jonathan A. Jones  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.09624v1>

Finally (f) shows how the average...</sub>

**Summary.** This work demonstrates that when shaping pulses for quantum control, restricting the control fields to vary only in phase (phase-only control) is computationally advantageous over controlling both amplitude and phase. The authors prove this equivalence by showing that complex amplitude modulations can be accurately approximated using sequences of phase-shifted sub-pulses. This simplifies the optimization process while maintaining high fidelity for quantum gate operations.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a highly efficient theoretical framework for designing complex quantum gates by restricting the control parameters to only phases, which simplifies optimization landscapes significantly compared to full amplitude/phase control.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper compares phase-only control versus full phase-and-amplitude control for designing shaped pulses using the GRAPE algorithm.

**Main result.** Phase-only optimization is shown to be computationally superior (faster and simpler) while achieving equivalent controllability to amplitude/phase methods.

**Method.** Optimal control theory, specifically utilizing the Gradient Ascent Pulse Engineering (GRAPE) algorithm for pulse design.

**Model / system.** The system involves controlling a spin-1/2 nucleus interacting with an RF field, described by time-dependent Hamiltonians. The control is applied via shaped pulses in the xy-plane.

**Key observables.** Infidelity (measure of deviation from ideal evolution), fidelity, and average power deposition.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Pulse amplitude ($\alpha$), frequency offset ($\Omega$), total pulse time ($	au$), and the number of sub-pulses used in approximations.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The equivalence between phase-only and full control is shown via approximation (Trotterization), which assumes certain commutation relations hold for the background Hamiltonian ($H_0$).

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate simulating amplitude modulation using multiple sub-pulses (Trotter approximations) and comparing the resulting fidelity/infidelity of phase-only vs. full control designs.

**Paper structure.** The paper first introduces the comparison between control methods, then details the mathematical equivalence via Trotterization, demonstrates the computational advantages of phase-only control, and concludes with practical implementation notes regarding pulse smoothing.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We compare phase-only control with phase-and-amplitude control when designing shaped pulses using the GRAPE algorithm, and explain why phase-only control has significant advantages. Trotterisation can be used to simulate amplitude modulation with phase modulation, indicating that the two approaches are fundamentally equivalent. Pulses designed by either method can be interconverted, but phase-only optimization is faster and simpler. The resulting pulses can then be converted into phase-and-amplitude form for more robust implementation if desired.

</details>

**Authors:** J. V. S Scursulim  
**Type:** both · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.08504v1>

Each curve corresponds to a different number of measurement shots, and each point represents the empirical success probability estimated over 100 independent runs, where success is defined as the optimal bitstring x∗having non-zero sampling probability after training with the corresponding number of objective function calls. **Summary.** This paper introduces a novel mixed Dicke state ansatz designed for Variational Quantum Eigensolvers tackling combinatorial optimization with hard equality and inequality constraints. By structurally encoding these constraints into the quantum circuit, it eliminates the need for penalty terms common in VQAs. The method is validated to outperform random search significantly as the problem complexity increases.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a concrete, structured method to embed complex physical constraints directly into quantum circuits, which is crucial for applying VQE to real-world constrained problems in quantum simulation or computation.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The core challenge is designing a variational quantum circuit ansatz expressive enough for constrained combinatorial optimization problems without relying on computationally expensive penalty terms.

**Main result.** The proposed mixed Dicke state ansatz successfully encodes both equality and inequality constraints directly into the quantum circuit, demonstrating clear advantages over random search as the feasible search space grows.

**Method.** The authors utilize a Variational Quantum Eigensolver (VQE) framework, adapting the density matrix formalism to construct a feasibility-preserving ansatz based on pure and mixed Dicke states.

**Model / system.** The problem is mapped onto finding the ground state energy of an Ising Hamiltonian. The quantum state space is explored using Dicke states, which are generalized via tensor products to handle multiple constraint groups.

**Key observables.** Probability of finding the optimal solution ($P(x = x^*)$), expectation value $\langle H angle$, and the reduced density matrix $\sigma(\vec{\alpha}, \vec{	heta})$.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Constraint complexity (number/type of constraints), number of objective function calls ($n_{	ext{calls}}$), and hardware noise levels.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The framework assumes that the optimal solution can be found by minimizing the expectation value over the constrained subspace, although achieving perfect convergence requires many iterations.

**Figures summary.** Figures compare the probability of finding the optimum versus objective function calls for VQE-Dicke against random search; another figure shows sampled portfolio points relative to the classical efficient frontier.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the need for constraint enforcement in VQAs, proposes the mixed Dicke state ansatz structure (extending pure states), validates this approach using combinatorial portfolio optimization across increasing complexity scenarios, and concludes with hardware performance analysis on NISQ devices.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Combinatorial optimization can be addressed with quantum computing through variational quantum algorithms, but a central challenge in this approach is to design an ansatz expressive enough to explore the feasible subspace of the Hilbert space where the optimal solution lies. Another major challenge is tuning the Lagrange multipliers in penalty terms to enforce feasibility and guarantee solution quality. To address both challenges, we propose the first feasibility-preserving mixed Dicke state ansatz for Hamming weight constrained combinatorial optimization, extending the density matrix formalism to structurally encode equality and inequality constraints directly into the quantum circuit, thereby eliminating the need for penalty terms in the objective function. The proposed framework handles both constraint types, with the pure Dicke state ansatz recovered as a special case corresponding to equality constraints, and generalizes to multiple constraint groups via tensor products of individual pure or mixed Dicke states. We validate the proposed approach in the context of combinatorial portfolio optimization across three experimental scenarios with increasing constraint complexity, using the CMA-ES optimizer and comparing its performance against random search with replacement restricted to the feasible subspace. As the feasible search space grows, the proposed ansatz demonstrates a clear advantage over random search in terms of the number of objective function calls required to identify high-quality solutions. Hardware experiments on IBM NISQ processors confirm that noise mitigation and circuit transpilation optimizations remain open challenges for practical deployment. The framework is general and directly applicable to other combinatorial optimization problems with Hamming weight constraints.

</details>

### [Quantum Cut Sparsifiers](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09728v1)

**Authors:** Arpon Basu, Joshua Brakensiek, Pravesh K. Kothari, Aaron Putterman  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.09728v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `analog quantum simulation` **1/5**

<img src="2606.09728_figures/2606.09728_page2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Low-resolution page preview, page 2</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2606.09728_figures/2606.09728_page3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Low-resolution page preview, page 3</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<sub>Low-resolution page preview, page 4</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2606.09728_figures/2606.09728_page5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Low-resolution page preview, page 5</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2606.09728_figures/2606.09728_page6.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Low-resolution page preview, page 6</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper develops methods to create highly efficient 'Quantum Cut sparsifiers' for QC Hamiltonians describing many-body quantum systems. It proves that these complex Hamiltonians can be represented by far fewer terms while maintaining spectral accuracy. This breakthrough has significant implications for the simulation and understanding of large, interacting quantum systems.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a powerful tool for analyzing complex quantum Hamiltonians by showing they can be accurately approximated using only a sparse set of interactions, which is crucial for simulating large quantum systems efficiently.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The primary goal is constructing an efficient, sparse representation (sparsifier) for Quantum Cut (QC) Hamiltonians associated with a general graph G.

**Main result.** The authors prove the existence of such a sparsifier $	ilde{L}_e^G$ whose size scales as $O(n/\epsilon^2)$ edges, preserving the energy spectrum up to $(1 \pm \epsilon)$.

**Method.** The technique relies on decomposing the action of the Hamiltonians into invariant subspaces and employing advanced operator-valued inequalities (like Alon-Kozma's) derived from harmonic analysis on hypercube slices.

**Model / system.** The system is defined by QC Hamiltonians, $L_G$, which are related to the Laplacian of a graph called the Kikuchi Graph ($KG$). These operators act on function spaces over subsets of vertices in an $n$-qubit system.

**Key observables.** Spectral approximation error ($\epsilon$), the size of the sparsifier $|E'|$, and the spectral gap relative to the full Hamiltonian.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The number of qubits ($n$) and the desired accuracy ($\epsilon$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Success relies on decomposing graphs into 'mild expanders' and utilizing advanced inequalities that overcome limitations faced by standard matrix concentration methods when local term importance is high.

**Paper structure.** The paper builds conceptually from classical cut-sparsifiers to the quantum setting, establishing the connection to Kikuchi graphs. The methodology involves spectral decomposition, applying advanced inequalities (Alon-Kozma), and generalizing results from complete/expander graphs to arbitrary weighted graphs via expander decomposition.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

In this paper, we continue a line of research initiated by Basu, Brakensiek, and Putterman [2026] studying the sparsifiability of Hamiltonians. We focus particularly on the sparsifiability of the widely-studied Quantum Cut (QC) Hamiltonians. Our main result is that in an $n$-qubit system, any $n$-qubit QC Hamiltonian can be sparsified to $\widetilde{O}(n /\varepsilon^2)$ many terms while preserving the energy of every state up to a factor of $1 \pm \varepsilon$. Our result can be interpreted as giving an importance sampling scheme for the edges of an arbitrary graph $G$ such that the \emph{Kikuchi} graph at level $\ell$ of the sampled graph is a spectral approximation to the Kikuchi graph of $G$. Importantly, the \emph{same} sampling scheme works simultaneously for all $\ell$.   The natural approach of leverage score sampling, analyzed via matrix concentration inequalities, yields a polynomially worse bound in our setting because the underlying matrices have dimension $\sim 2^n$. Instead, our approach relies on decomposing the action of these matrices into invariant subspaces. Then, by using an operator-valued inequality of Alon and Kozma [Ann. Henri Poincaré, 2020], itself building on an \emph{octopus inequality} of Caputo, Liggett, and Richthammer [J. AMS, 2010], we extend our sparsification technique to all expander graphs. We then invoke expander decomposition to extend our sparsifier to all graphs.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper introduces Quantum Global Variational Learning (QGVL) as an efficient alternative to standard QAE methods for Quantum Error Correction. By consolidating local unitaries into a global structure, QGVL drastically reduces training complexity and time while maintaining high fidelity in correcting errors across various qubit codes. It demonstrates that this ML approach can achieve performance comparable to established theoretical stabilizer codes.

**Why it may be interesting.** For AMO physicists studying open quantum systems, this work provides a computational framework to model how machine learning can be used to design or optimize error mitigation protocols for physical hardware limited by decoherence.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Developing efficient Quantum Error Correction (QEC) methods that avoid classical measurements and overcome training convergence issues inherent in large quantum neural networks.

**Main result.** The proposed Quantum Global Variational Learning (QGVL) approach significantly reduces the number of required unitary parameters compared to standard QAE, leading to substantial improvements in training time and robustness against internal network noise while matching established stabilizer code performance.

**Method.** The authors propose using a global structure within a quantum neural network framework for error correction. Training involves minimizing infidelity by optimizing trainable unitary matrices via gradient descent techniques adapted for quantum circuits.

**Model / system.** The system models qubits susceptible to various noise channels, including Pauli X errors and general depolarizing noise. QEC is tested using established codes like the 3-qubit repetition code, 5-qubit, Steane (7-qubit), and Shor (9-qubit) stabilizer codes.

**Key observables.** Mean fidelity of error correction output vs. input error rate ($p$), training time reduction (e.g., 80% for 3-qubit), and success rate in achieving convergence.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Error rates ($p$, $p_n$), number of qubits ($n=3, 5, 7, 9$), and network structure parameters ($m, n$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis often assumes that noise affecting different quantum states is equally probable when modeling internal network noise. The comparison relies on comparing QGVL performance against established algorithmic stabilizer codes.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate fidelity dependence on error rate for various qubit counts (3, 5, 7, 9) comparing QGVL to stabilizer codes; diagrams show the layered QNN architecture and the global unitary mapping structure.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from establishing the need for efficient QEC over measurement-based methods. It contrasts Quantum Autoencoders (QAE) with the proposed Global Variational Learning (QGVL), demonstrating QGVL's parameter efficiency, and finally validates its performance across increasing qubit counts and complex noise models.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Efficient quantum error correction is essential for the advancement of quantum computing. We propose a quantum neural network with a global structure that reduces the number of unitary matrices required in quantum circuits. This approach resulted in a 97\% reduction in training time and up to a 25\% improvement in the training completion rate, ultimately achieving a 100\% success rate in training while surpassing the error correction performance reported in previous studies. In addition, we demonstrated the enhanced robustness of quantum error correction against internal network noise. Moreover, the fidelity of quantum error correction under internal network noise increased by up to 15\% due to the reduced computational load.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

**Summary.** This paper analyzes the performance of various Quantum Key Distribution protocols for satellite-based quantum links, accounting for realistic atmospheric distortions. It compares standard QKD methods against high-dimensional extensions, concluding that higher dimensions offer superior robustness in noisy space channels. The findings guide protocol selection for future LEO quantum communication networks.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work directly addresses the practical implementation challenges of quantum communication over long distances by rigorously modeling atmospheric channel impairments and comparing encoding schemes (2D vs HD).

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The primary goal is to perform a comprehensive performance evaluation of Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) protocols specifically designed for satellite-based quantum communication links.

**Main result.** High-Dimensional QKD protocol HD-BB84 was shown to achieve higher key rates and superior noise tolerance compared to HD-Extended B92 under realistic atmospheric conditions.

**Method.** The analysis employs detailed numerical simulations using a circular/elliptic beam propagation model that incorporates complex atmospheric effects like diffraction, turbulence, attenuation, and pointing errors.

**Model / system.** The system modeled is the free-space quantum channel between Low Earth Orbit (LEO) satellites and ground stations. Protocols evaluated include BB84, B92, BBM92, E91, and their high-dimensional generalizations.

**Key observables.** Key rate performance, Quantum Bit Error Rate (QBER), Probability Distribution of Key Rate (PDR), and conditional von Neumann entropy $S(a|E)$.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Atmospheric parameters ($C_n^2$), zenith angle ($\phi$), link length ($L$), and the depolarizing channel parameter ($q$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on approximations such as the elliptic-beam approximation for turbulence effects and models environmental noise contributions (e.g., using a depolarizing channel).

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate key rate and QBER variations as functions of zenith angle, link length, and under different weather conditions for various protocols.

**Paper structure.** The paper builds from theoretical foundations (qubits, entanglement) to protocol evaluation. It systematically compares standard vs. high-dimensional protocols across varying atmospheric/operational regimes, culminating in detailed rate derivations using entropy bounds.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) has emerged as a fundamentally secure approach to communication in the era of quantum computing, offering protection against threats posed to classical cryptographic schemes such as RSA and Diffie-Hellman. This thesis presents a comprehensive performance analysis of satellite-based QKD protocols, focusing on both prepare-and-measure and entanglement-based schemes under realistic atmospheric and operational conditions. The study begins by introducing the theoretical foundations of quantum communication, including qubits, entanglement, and quantum entropy, and motivates the need for satellite-based QKD to overcome the distance limitations of fiber-based systems. Subsequently, the thesis evaluates four prominent QKD protocols-BB84, B92, BBM92, and E91-using a circular beam propagation model that incorporates atmospheric effects such as diffraction, turbulence, attenuation, and pointing errors, along with environmental noise contributions for uplink and downlink. Comparative numerical simulations reveal that protocol performance is strongly influenced by channel asymmetries, beam propagation characteristics, and noise, providing guidance on optimal protocol selection for low Earth orbit (LEO) satellite links. The research further investigates high-dimensional (HD) QKD protocols, specifically HD-BB84 and HD-Extended B92, using the elliptic-beam approximation to account for turbulence-induced distortions for both uplink and downlink. Simulations under vary ing system dimensions, weather conditions, and zenith angles demonstrate that HD-BB84 achieves higher key rates, superior noise tolerance, and more favorable probability distributions of the key rate compared to HD-Extended B92, highlighting the advantages of high-dimensional encoding for robust satellite-based QKD.

</details>

### [Simulating quantum circuits with a neural statebank](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08707v1)

**Authors:** Taige Wang, Liang Fu  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** numerical methods · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.08707v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `methods for driven-dissipative` **1/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2606.08707_figures/2606.08707_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1. Statebank training. A circuit is decomposed into layers U1, U2, . . ., which produce intermediate wavefunc- tions |ψ0⟩, |ψ1⟩, . . .. Instead of storing these states as full 2N-component vectors, the simulator represents each one by an autoregressive Transformer checkpoint. To train checkpoint θℓ, samples and amplitudes from θℓ−1 are combined with the exact local action of Uℓto define targets for the next state.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2606.08707_figures/2606.08707_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 2. HCZCH benchmark. a, HCZCH circuit shown for N = 8 qubits. b, Representative layer-by-layer training diagnostics. Diagonal layers are monitored by phase error, whereas CH and CNOT layers are monitored by local-unitary overlap error. c, Memory use. Exact state-vector storage reaches 128 GiB at N = 34, while the Transformer statebank remains near 3 GiB over the widths studied. d, Final infidelity. The statebank remains near 10−2 from N = 22 to 34, whereas MPS and near-Clifford perturbation deteriorate at larger width. e, Amplitude-query time. Tensor-network contraction is competitive for selected amplitudes, but the statebank gives fastest amortized queries after training. f,...</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2606.08707_figures/2606.08707_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 3(a) and 3(b) benchmark the quantum approxi- mate optimization algorithm (QAOA) at p = 2, a central variational circuit family for near-term optimization and quantum algorithm design [45–50]. At generic angles, QAOA is non-Clifford, providing a natural test beyond stabilizer-based simulation. The statebank treats the di- agonal cost layers as phase updates and the mixer layers as one-qubit local updates. The fidelity remains close to unity from N = 22 to 34.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2606.08707_figures/2606.08707_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 3(c) and 3(d) test a two-block H-CNOT-T circuit. This family is important because H, CNOT, and T form a standard universal gate set [51–54]. The same check- pointing procedure learns the branching induced by H and the basis mixing induced by CNOT, while treating the T layers as diagonal phase updates.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2606.08707_figures/2606.08707_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 3(e) and 3(f) use a purely Clifford circuit as a control. This is not a regime in which a neural simulator should be expected to outperform specialized stabilizer</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** The paper proposes a neural statebank architecture to overcome the exponential scaling problem in simulating quantum circuits. By training autoregressive Transformers layer-by-layer, it creates a compact neural representation of the wavefunction trajectory. This method achieves high fidelity on large, complex circuits while drastically reducing memory requirements compared to exact simulation.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a novel, memory-efficient computational tool to simulate quantum dynamics that goes beyond the limitations of both full state-vector storage and specialized tensor network methods for highly entangled or structured circuits.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Predicting the output of quantum circuits is computationally intractable due to the exponential growth of the wavefunction, creating a bottleneck for verifying quantum processors.

**Main result.** The proposed neural statebank successfully simulates complex quantum circuits up to 34 qubits with low infidelity while requiring significantly less memory than exact state-vector evolution.

**Method.** It introduces an autoregressive Transformer checkpoint that learns the wavefunction trajectory layer by layer, approximating the full circuit evolution using a compact neural representation.

**Model / system.** The model simulates quantum circuits acting on N qubits. The system is characterized by its state vector $|\psiangle$ and its evolution through sequential unitary gates ($U = U_D \cdots U_1$).

**Key observables.** Infidelity (error rate) of the simulation, memory scaling relative to qubit number (N), and computational time.

**Important parameters / regimes.** System size (N up to 34 qubits), circuit depth/number of layers, entanglement structure (magic gates, non-diagonal branching).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The method assumes that the state evolution can be accurately captured by an autoregressive neural network trained sequentially on local gate updates. The primary limitation is accumulated error over deep circuits.

**Figures summary.** Figures compare memory usage and final infidelity across various simulators (State-Vector, MPS, Statebank) on demanding benchmarks like HCZCH; other figures benchmark the method on QAOA and Clifford circuits.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the statebank concept by addressing the exponential scaling problem. It details the autoregressive Transformer architecture for checkpointing, presents training procedures using local gate updates, and validates its performance against established simulators across diverse circuit families (HCZCH, QAOA, etc.).

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Predicting the output of quantum circuits is a central bottleneck for verifying quantum processors because a generic wavefunction grows exponentially with system size. We introduce a neural statebank that learns this wavefunction along the circuit trajectory. Each layer is stored as an autoregressive Transformer checkpoint trained from local gate updates to the preceding checkpoint, producing a compact neural representation that can evaluate amplitudes and generate independent samples. On long-range circuits combining entanglement, magic, and non-diagonal branching, a 0.3-million-parameter statebank reaches $\sim 10^{-2}$ infidelity at 34 qubits, outperforming the other tested approximate simulators while using far less memory than exact state-vector evolution. The same architecture accurately simulates quantum approximate optimization, Clifford+$T$, and Clifford circuits.

</details>

**Summary.** The paper proposes the Transformation-Response Framework (TRF), reformulating quantum mechanics by defining the state not as a vector in Hilbert space, but as a catalog of its complex responses ($\chi$) to all physical transformations. By postulating that this response function must be positive-definite, the authors derive the Born rule, Schrödinger equation, and path integrals as theorems, while also introducing 'product order positivity' as a potentially testable constraint.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant as it attempts to provide a foundational, axiomatic overhaul of quantum theory that bypasses canonical quantization procedures by rooting everything in measurable operational responses.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses conceptual tensions in standard quantum mechanics by seeking a logically economical, operationally grounded, and background-independent reformulation.

**Main result.** It derives the entire standard formalism of QM (Born rule, Schrödinger equation, path integral) from a single postulate: that the state is defined by a positive-definite response function $\chi$ to all physical transformations.

**Method.** The core method involves defining the quantum state operationally as the characteristic function $\chi(g)$ on the group $G$ of physical transformations, and then using functional analysis (like GNS construction and Bochner's theorem) to derive standard QM postulates from this single constraint.

**Model / system.** The model is a general quantum system whose symmetries are described by a transformation group $G$. The framework applies universally, aiming to cover everything from particle physics (Poincaré group) to background-independent gravity.

**Key observables.** The characteristic function $\chi(g)$, which catalogs the system's response to all transformations $g \in G$.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The fundamental constant $\hbar$, which emerges uniquely from consistency requirements across subgroups; time is treated as a coordinate along an automorphism subgroup.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The primary assumption is that physical reality can be entirely characterized by measurable responses to operations ($\chi$ must be positive-definite). A secondary, crucial constraint introduced is 'product order positivity' for non-abelian groups.

**Figures summary.** Not specified in the notes; the structure relies on mathematical derivations involving group algebras and functional analysis.

**Paper structure.** The paper systematically derives QM postulates: first establishing the state via $\chi(g)$ (Chunk 1/2), then constructing Hilbert space and deriving the Born rule (GNS/Bochner, Chunk 3), generalizing dynamics to automorphisms (Chunk 4), extending this to path integrals for QFT (Chunk 5), and finally introducing the novel constraint of product order positivity for non-abelian groups (Chunk 6).

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We present the transformation-response framework, an operational reformulation of quantum mechanics. A quantum state is not a Hilbert space object but the catalog of a system's responses to all physical transformations: for each operation $g$ from the system's local group $G$, an interference experiment gives a complex value $χ(g)$. The collection $\{χ(g): g\in G \}$ is the characteristic function and defines the state. The only postulate is that $χ$ is positive-definite, encoding the requirement that no superposition of transformations yields negative probability. From this single assumption, the entire standard formalism is derived: Hilbert space via GNS construction, Born rule via Bochner theorem, Schrödinger equation from group automorphisms, and especially Feynman path integral as a Trotter limit. The framework is background-independent and time-neutral: time is a coordinate along a one-parameter subgroup of $G$. It also reveals a new physical constraint, product order positivity, which may lead to testable predictions. The framework provides a unified, economical, and falsifiable foundation for quantum theory rooted in operational primitives.

</details>

Same code is produced by Algorithm 1 directly.</sub>

**Summary.** This paper presents a comprehensive algebraic theory for constructing Bivariate-Bicycle Surface Codes (BBS codes). It shows that the code's dimension and structure are determined by counting the common roots of two defining bivariate polynomials. The key contribution is a general construction algorithm applicable to complex, tilted boundaries without needing manual boundary fixes.

**Why it may be interesting.** While focused on abstract algebra, the rigorous determination of code parameters based purely on polynomial root counting provides deep insights into the topological constraints governing quantum error correction codes, which is fundamental to building fault-tolerant quantum computers.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper develops a general algebraic framework for constructing Bivariate-Bicycle Surface (BBS) codes, relating their parameters to the common roots of defining bivariate polynomials over a finite field.

**Main result.** A robust construction prescription is provided that determines the code dimension and distance by counting the number of finite, non-zero common roots of the defining polynomials, applicable even for arbitrarily tilted boundaries without corner corrections.

**Method.** The analysis heavily employs algebraic coding theory, utilizing concepts like chain complexes, Smith Normal Form invariants, resultants, and analyzing polynomial factorization properties over Laurent polynomial rings.

**Model / system.** The model is the Bivariate-Bicycle Surface Code (BBS code), which are quantum error-correcting codes defined on a lattice structure using stabilizer generators derived from pairs of bivariate polynomials $a(x, y)$ and $b(x, y)$.

**Key observables.** Code dimension ($k$), minimum distance ($d$), the number of common finite roots of $a$ and $b$, and topological invariants like $k_{TO}$.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The defining polynomials $a(x, y)$ and $b(x, y)$, the field $\mathbb{F}_q$, and boundary conditions (e.g., rectangular vs. tilted).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes specific edge conditions must be met for the general construction to hold without needing ad-hoc corner corrections; it also relies on the bulk condition being satisfied.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate planar layouts of BB codes, examples of boundary structures (smooth/rough), and visualizations of code generators derived from polynomials $a$ and $b$.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses by first establishing the connection between code dimension and common roots; it then details algebraic machinery using chain complexes and SNF invariants to analyze solutions in strips; finally, it generalizes this construction to arbitrary boundaries and compares its results against existing methods.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We relate the properties of bivariate-bicycle-surface (BBS) codes, constructed from a pair of bivariate polynomials over a finite field, to the number and location of their common roots in the extension field. The number of roots $(x,y)$ with finite, non-zero coordinates -- counted with algebraic multiplicity -- determines the dimension of the codes. This dimension is invariant under monomial automorphisms of the Laurent polynomial ring. Conversely, roots with zero or infinite $x$- or $y$-coordinates indicate that specialized generators are required near the corresponding boundary (e.g., the left or right boundary for a root where $x$ is zero or infinite, respectively). These roots can appear or disappear under monomial transformations, which reveals the structure of tilted boundaries. Based on these results, we formulate a prescription for constructing BBS codes that works for regions with rectangular, diagonal, and arbitrarily tilted boundaries. A key advantage of this approach is that no corner corrections are needed, provided the polynomials satisfy orientation-specific edge conditions.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper mathematically compares the standard power-series method against an alternative wavefunction ansatz for solving the Schrödinger equation for molecular potentials like the pseudoharmonic and Kratzer-Fues forms. The authors argue that their standard power-series approach is superior because it is simpler and yields general results via a two-term recurrence relation, unlike the more complex method they critique.

**Why it may be interesting.** This is highly relevant as it deals directly with solving fundamental quantum mechanical equations for molecular systems, which is core to AMO physics. The critique of alternative methods provides valuable insight into rigorous solution techniques.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper compares two mathematical techniques for solving the Schrödinger equation when applied to molecular potentials.

**Main result.** The standard power-series method is demonstrated to be mathematically simpler and more powerful than an alternative wavefunction ansatz proposed by Ikhdair and Sever.

**Method.** The analysis involves deriving recurrence relations for coefficients arising from series solutions to the radial Schrödinger equation, comparing two distinct formalisms.

**Model / system.** The study focuses on solving the eigenvalue problem for diatomic molecules using specific potentials: the pseudoharmonic potential and the Kratzer-Fues potential. The underlying physics is governed by the radial part of the Schrödinger equation after the Born-Oppenheimer approximation.

**Key observables.** Energy eigenvalues (E) and the corresponding eigenfunctions R(r).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The reduced mass ($\mu$) of the nuclei, and quantum numbers determining the state.

**Assumptions / limitations.** It assumes that physically acceptable solutions must arise from truncating the infinite power series to yield a polynomial solution.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem by stating the comparison between two methods. It then applies these methods to specific potentials (pseudoharmonic and Kratzer-Fues) to derive recurrence relations, ultimately concluding with a direct comparison of the mathematical utility of the two approaches.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We show that the standard power-series method described in many textbooks of quantum-mechanics and quantum-chemistry is simpler and more powerful than the wavefunction approach proposed by Ikhdair and Sever [Cent. Eur. J. Phys. 6, 697 (2008)]. As illustrative examples we choose the pseudoharmonic and Kratzer-Fues potentials already treated by those authors.

</details>

### [QnRL: Quantum-Native Reinforcement Learning](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08276v1)

**Authors:** Alexander DeRieux, Walid Saad  
**Type:** both · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.08276v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2606.08276_figures/2606.08276_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIGURE 1. General design of our QnRL framework. A QnRL agent generates superimposed quantum conditional return distributions (blue) for all actions given an environment observation, and then runs QuAK algorithm (green) to derive an action distribution relative to the normalized n-th power of the m-th moment of each conditional return distribution. Action selection (white) is performed by measuring the action qubits. During training, the agent updates the quantum distributional model by generating discounted return distributions for selected target actions (orange), realigns the atoms of this target distribution (red), and then computes policy loss relative to this target distribution...</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2606.08276_figures/2606.08276_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIGURE 2. High-level view of action selection process in QnRL using QuAK algorithm. The agent generates a superimposed quantum state (dashed orange) that is the distribution of returns conditioned on an input observation for every action. The agent then applies a nonlinear value map via Kraus operator (yellow) followed by a quantum reset operation (purple) to map the superimposed distributions of returns into a distribution of actions, where the probability of each action is proportional to the normalized n-th power of the m-th moment of its return distribution. The agent then measures the action qubits (white) to determine the best action.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2606.08276_figures/2606.08276_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIGURE 3. Quantum circuit for our proposed conditional return distribution generator U π z | x,a, which has cascaded layers of encoding (green), entanglement (yellow), and variational (blue) operators, followed by a final QFT (purple). In this work we use two variations of entanglement, which are circular entanglement (left), and layer offset entanglement (right) respectively.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2606.08276_figures/2606.08276_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIGURE 4. Quantum circuit for QuAK algorithm (gray). An initial action distribution (green) is used to seed the cascaded generation of conditional return distributions (orange), followed by cascaded nonlinear return-value maps via Kraus operator (red), and a final reset over the return qubits (purple) to generate an action distribution that is proportional to the normalized n-th power of the m-th moment of each respective return distribution. Measurements on the action qubits (white) sample from this weighted distribution.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2606.08276_figures/2606.08276_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIGURE 5. Comparison of CartPole metrics (a) train episode reward, (b) evaluation episode reward, and (c) model parameter count for best model configuration of C51 (orange) and QnRL (blue). Training performance averaged over 4 seeds, evaluation performance averaged over 10 seeds, with ±1 std. dev. shown. These figures generally show that QnRL achieves higher and more stable training and evaluation performance with a significantly fewer parameters.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper introduces Quantum-Native Reinforcement Learning (QnRL), a framework that overcomes the limitations of classical and prior quantum RL by representing probability distributions as complex quantum states. It utilizes a novel QuAK algorithm to analyze these distributions directly in Hilbert space, leading to superior performance across diverse simulated environments.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a concrete methodology for embedding complex statistical learning problems (like DRL) directly into quantum circuit operations, offering insights into how quantum information theory can solve computational bottlenecks in AI.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Existing Quantum Reinforcement Learning (QRL) methods limit expressive power by only estimating expected outcomes, failing to model the full probability distributions governing stochastic environments.

**Main result.** The proposed QnRL framework achieves significantly higher evaluation scores and requires fewer parameters than baselines by natively modeling conditional return distributions in Hilbert space.

**Method.** It introduces Quantum-Native Reinforcement Learning (QnRL), which uses a novel Quantum Amplitude Kickback (QuAK) algorithm to analyze moments of superimposed quantum state distributions directly within the complex Hilbert space.

**Model / system.** The system extends classical DRL by representing return and policy distributions as superimposed, entangled quantum states in a combined action-return Hilbert space ($H_A \otimes H_Z$). The core mechanism involves Variational Quantum Circuits (VQC) for distribution generation.

**Key observables.** Evaluation scores, expected total reward, conditional action policy distribution $P(A|x)$, and the ability to capture complex correlations via phase/amplitude components.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Action qubits ($q_A$), return qubits ($q_Z$), moment order ($m$), expectation exponent ($n$), circuit depth ($L$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The primary assumption is that quantum computation can provide an advantage by keeping the entire distributional learning process within the complex Hilbert space, avoiding classical measurement sampling loss.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the four core stages of QnRL: Return distribution generation (superposition), moment analysis via QuAK, action selection based on moments, and final model optimization.

**Paper structure.** The paper details the system model expansion from classical to quantum settings, introduces the QnRL framework and the QuAK algorithm in dedicated sections, presents comprehensive results across multiple challenging environments, and concludes with detailed hyperparameter comparisons.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Quantum reinforcement learning (QRL) is a promising approach to learn effective decision strategies across several applications with stochastic environments. Instead of directly modeling the random variables that govern these environments, existing QRL architectures indirectly approximate environment behavior by estimating expected outcomes, which limits their expressive power and adaptive potential. Overcoming such challenges requires a novel QRL approach that exploits the distributional nature of quantum computers to directly model environment random variables as quantum state distributions. Hence, in this paper, a novel framework dubbed quantum-native reinforcement learning (QnRL) is proposed. QnRL is a distributional RL framework that learns conditional distributions naturally in Hilbert space via superimposed and entangled quantum states. Thus, QnRL can directly model the behavior of stochastic learning environments via the natural properties of quantum systems. QnRL accomplishes this via a novel, proposed quantum amplitude kickback (QuAK) algorithm that enables comparing the $n$-th power of the $m$-th moment of multiple superimposed distributions. It is theoretically proven that a conditional action policy distribution is distilled from the moments of a quantum generative model entirely within Hilbert space via QuAK, and optimized via QnRL. This complex distribution composition is also shown to provide extra dimensions for expressing environment correlations that are unknown to purely classical and classically-sampled quantum distributional models. Experimental results across diverse environments show that QnRL achieves up to $82.9\%$ higher evaluation scores, with up to $94.3\%$ fewer parameters on average, more accurately estimates the expected return for unseen observations, and better adapts to varying stochastic conditions compared to the baseline.

</details>

**Authors:** Chaowen Guan, Akshit Katiyar  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.08443v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

**Summary.** The paper presents an efficient quantum algorithm for the Quantum Kravchuk Transform by exploiting its deep mathematical connection to the $\mathfrak{su}(2)$ Lie algebra. By mapping the transform onto this algebraic structure and utilizing fast-forwarding simulation techniques, the authors achieve a polylogarithmic gate complexity. This demonstrates a powerful methodology for implementing transforms associated with discrete orthogonal polynomials on quantum hardware.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a concrete example of applying advanced algebraic structures ($\mathfrak{su}(2)$) and state-of-the-art quantum simulation techniques (fast-forwarding) to solve a problem in orthogonal polynomial theory, demonstrating potential quantum advantage over classical methods for specific transforms.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The primary goal is developing an efficient quantum algorithm for the Quantum Kravchuk Transform (QKT). This aims to achieve a computational complexity that scales logarithmically with system size, surpassing classical polynomial scaling.

**Main result.** An efficient quantum circuit implementing the QKT was developed by mapping the transform structure onto the $\mathfrak{su}(2)$ Lie algebra. The resulting algorithm achieves a time complexity of $O((\log N + \log(1/\epsilon))^3 	imes \log(1/\epsilon))$ gates.

**Method.** The method combines establishing a structural link between the Kravchuk transform and $\mathfrak{su}(2)$, followed by applying advanced fast-forwarding simulation techniques for $	ext{SU}(2)$ group elements in the oscillator representation.

**Model / system.** The system is based on the discrete harmonic oscillator, whose eigenfunctions are the Kravchuk polynomials/functions defined over a finite lattice $X_N = \{0, 1, \dots, N\}$. The dynamics and transform properties are modeled using operators satisfying $\mathfrak{su}(2)$ commutation relations.

**Key observables.** The QKT itself (mapping computational basis states to Kravchuk function states), the spectrum of the discrete harmonic oscillator Hamiltonian ($\hat{K}$), and the matrix elements of the transformation operators.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Dimension $N$ (or lattice size), error bound $\epsilon$, and the logarithmic scaling factors derived from the fast-forwarding techniques.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The approach relies on successfully mapping the Kravchuk transform structure onto an irreducible representation of $\mathfrak{su}(2)$ to enable the use of established efficient simulation algorithms for Lie groups.

**Paper structure.** The paper builds by first defining the mathematical properties of the Kravchuk polynomials and their connection to the discrete harmonic oscillator. It then establishes this link to $\mathfrak{su}(2)$ via the Jordan-Schwinger map, allowing the application of fast-forwarding techniques developed for $	ext{SU}(2)$ evolution to construct the final quantum circuit.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We present a quantum algorithm for the Kravchuk transform that scales logarithmically in both the dimension and the inverse of the error parameter. The quantum Kravchuk transform maps computational basis states to states with amplitudes proportional to Kravchuk functions. We achieve this by combining two key techniques: the structural relationship between the Kravchuk transform and the Lie algebras $\mathfrak{su}(2)$, and a recent fast-forwarding simulation method for $\mathfrak{su}(2)$ operators in the oscillator representation. More precisely, we first establish the map from Kravchuk transform in computational basis to $\mathfrak{su}(2)$ in Fock basis. Then built on this connection, we apply the fast-forwarding to achieve an efficient quantum Kravchuk transform.

</details>

<a id="paper-2606.07757"></a>
### [REM universality and Poisson-Dirichlet Gibbs weights for linear random energy](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07757v1)

**Authors:** Francesco Concetti, Simone Franchini  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.07757v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2606.07757_figures/2606.07757_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 1. The curve 𝐺∗denotes the quenched large-deviation rate function of 𝐻𝑛conditional on the environment ℎ. The parameter ˜𝑎is defined by 𝐺∗( ˜𝑎) = 𝑐𝜌, and ˜𝜆is the slope of 𝐺∗at ˜𝑎.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper proves that the energy levels of a purely linear random Hamiltonian exhibit Random Energy Model (REM) universality after thinning. It rigorously determines that the resulting point process converges to a Poisson Point Process and identifies that the associated Gibbs weights converge to the Poisson-Dirichlet law, revealing a phase transition in the free energy at a critical temperature.

**Why it may be interesting.** The rigorous proof of REM universality for this specific linear model provides deep insights into how complex statistical physics models simplify to universal limiting forms, which is relevant for understanding disordered systems in quantum mechanics.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study aims to establish Random Energy Model (REM) universality for energy levels derived from a purely linear random Hamiltonian and determine the limiting behavior of associated Gibbs weights.

**Main result.** The thinned point process of energy levels converges to a Poisson Point Process, and the joint distribution of ranked Gibbs weights converges to the Poisson-Dirichlet law in the low-temperature regime.

**Method.** The analysis uses techniques involving independent thinning processes, computing limit Laplace functionals for point processes, and applying large-deviation principles to analyze weight convergence.

**Model / system.** The system is defined by the Hamiltonian H_n(h, \sigma) = \sum_{i=1}^n h_i(\sigma_i - m), where h_i are i.i.d. random variables and \sigma_i are Ising spins. The analysis focuses on energy levels obtained after retaining an exponential number of configurations.

**Key observables.** Energy level point process, Gibbs weights, quenched free energy F(β).

**Important parameters / regimes.** β (inverse temperature), τλ (critical parameter related to the PPP intensity), and the thinning factor c_ό.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The proof relies on regularity hypotheses for h_1, including a linear small-ball condition, and assumes sharp large-deviation estimates from prior work.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 illustrates the quenched large-deviation rate function G*(a) versus a, defining parameters τλ and α.

**Paper structure.** The paper first establishes REM universality for the point process convergence to PPP(D_{τλ}), then analyzes the Gibbs weights showing convergence to PD(τλ/β, 0), and finally computes the free energy exhibiting a phase transition at β = τλ.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We study the Hamiltonian $H_n(h,σ)=\sum_{i=1}^n h_i(σ_i-m), $ where $(h_i)$ are i.i.d.\ real random variables and $(σ_i)$ are i.i.d.\ Ising spins. We consider the energy levels obtained after an independent thinning that retains an exponential number of configurations ($e^{O(n)}$). We prove that, after an $(h_i)$-dependent centering, the resulting point process converges in distribution to a Poisson point process with exponential intensity. Thus, the energy levels asymptotically has the one of the Random Energy Model (REM). Our results extend previous ones, where REM universality for this model was established only either for energy fluctuations of order $e^{-O(n)}$ or for $e^{o(\sqrt n)}$ randomly selected configurations. We also identify the limiting Gibbs weights, which converge to a Poisson--Dirichlet law, and the quenched free energy, which exhibits a freezing transition at $β=\tildeλ$. The proofs are presented here in compressed form; full details are given in the companion preprint.

</details>

**Authors:** Sølve Selstø, Tor Kristian Dahle, Sergiy Denysov, Yves-Laurent Ariel Rezus, Leiv Øyehaug

**Summary.** This work reformulates Zermelo's river-crossing problem as an adiabatic quantum computation task using qutrits. By analyzing the system's evolution through avoided crossings via the Landau-Zener formula, the authors demonstrate that a seemingly complex optimization problem possesses an efficient classical solution scaling only quadratically with size. This highlights how quantum tools can reveal underlying classical structure.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a pedagogical example of mapping a real-world optimization problem onto a quantum annealer framework, allowing for analytical estimation of performance metrics like fidelity using established quantum dynamics tools.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses Zermelo's navigation problem, a classic optimization challenge, by formulating it as an adiabatic quantum computation task.

**Main result.** Despite the initial appearance of exponential complexity, the quantum formulation reveals an underlying classical structure that can be solved efficiently with only quadratic scaling in the problem size.

**Method.** The solution utilizes adiabatic quantum computing principles, estimating fidelity via the Landau-Zener formula to analyze the evolution through avoided crossings.

**Model / system.** The system models a discretized path across a river using qutrits (ternary variables) encoded into a Hamiltonian. The total Hamiltonian combines a cost term and a penalty term enforcing boundary conditions.

**Key observables.** Fidelity ($F$ or $F_{LZ}$), required annealing time ($T_{pen}$), and the optimal path $\vec{s}_{opt}$.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The problem size $n$, the annealing schedule parameters ($\alpha(t), eta(t)$), and the critical crossing values.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The validity of the Landau-Zener approximation, which assumes negligible interference effects in the large time limit ($T_{pen} 	o \infty$).

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the optimal path (Fig. 1), discretized steps (Fig. 2), the three-phase annealing schedule (Fig. 3), and comparisons between actual vs. Landau-Zener fidelity versus $T_{pen}$ (Fig. 6).

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces Zermelo's problem, maps it to a qutrit Hamiltonian ($H = H_{	ext{cost}} + eta H_{	ext{pen}}$), details the adiabatic evolution using an annealing schedule, analyzes the fidelity via Landau-Zener theory, and concludes by deriving efficient classical algorithms that match the quantum prediction.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The river-crossing problem, also known as Zermelo's navigation problem, is a classic example of an optimization problem with practical relevance and a scalable degree of complexity. It asks for the optimal trajectory of a vessel moving through a water flow field and provides a setting in which physics, variational methods, and optimization are naturally intertwined. We state a version of Zermelo's problem and then solve it as formulate it as an adiabatic quantum-computing problem using quantum trits, or qutrits for short. The construction includes a penalty term that enforces the prescribed boundary conditions and an exploration term that allows the system to move through intermediate configurations toward the optimal feasible path. In the adiabatic description, the evolution proceeds through a sequence of avoided crossings, so that the resulting fidelity can be estimated using the Landau-Zener formula. Remarkably, the regime in which this approximation is valid also provides a deterministic way to identify the correct solution with computational effort that scales only quadratically with the problem size. Thus, a quantum formulation initially motivated by the apparent exponential complexity of the problem reveals an underlying classical structure that can be exploited efficiently. Our approach also provides a pedagogical illustration of how a real-world optimization problem can be cast into a quantum-annealing framework and then analyzed using the Schrödinger equation, avoided crossings, and Landau-Zener theory.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper characterizes an advanced optoelectronic magnonic parametric oscillator built around a Mach-Zehnder interferometer. By analyzing the system's response to magnetic field perturbations, the authors demonstrate that the MZI enables complex, adaptive dynamics beyond simple resonance locking. This framework is valuable for exploring nonlinear interferometric physics in hybrid magnonic-photonic systems.

**Why it may be interesting.** The work provides an excellent platform for studying open quantum systems dynamics by modeling coherent phase redistribution and adaptive nonlinear interactions between two distinct physical subsystems (magnonics and photonics).

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study investigates adaptive interferometric oscillator dynamics under distributed phase perturbations using a hybrid magnonic-photonic platform.

**Main result.** The MZI architecture introduces an additional adaptive degree of freedom, leading to partially synchronized dynamics where the YIG branch acts locally and the phase shifter controls global geometry.

**Method.** Analysis involves measuring frequency pulling, comparing local vs. global sampling configurations, and applying complex-Lorentzian fitting techniques.

**Model / system.** The system is an MZI-based optoelectronic magnonic parametric oscillator (OEMPO) combining a YIG-loaded magnonic branch and a tunable phase-shifter branch within an OEO loop structure.

**Key observables.** Loop Free Spectral Range (FSR), effective delay time ($	au$), cross-susceptibility coefficient ($\kappa_{YIG	o ps}$), and phase-to-amplitude conversion ratios.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The system exhibits distinct behavior in the phase-pinned OEPO regime versus the frequency-adaptive OEO regime.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes that the cross-coupling can be separated into amplitude-mediated and phase-mediated terms, with the latter dominating the coupling mechanism.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate schematics of the setup, measurements comparing FSR determination via mode pair separation (OEPO) versus frequency pulling (OEO), and phase evolution vs. magnetic field for different sampling points.

**Paper structure.** The paper systematically analyzes the system by first establishing the MZI structure's role in introducing adaptive freedom, then quantifying the distinct roles of the YIG branch (local dispersion) and the PS branch (global geometry), culminating in a quantitative analysis of their cross-coupling and regime differences.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We study a Mach-Zehnder interferometer (MZI)-based optoelectronic magnonic parametric oscillator (OEMPO) incorporating a YIG-loaded magnonic branch and a tunable phase-shifter branch, enabling systematic investigation of adaptive interferometric oscillator dynamics under distributed phase perturbations. Through analysis of nondegenerate OEPO mode pairs and frequency-pulling behavior, the loop free spectral range (FSR) and effective delay time were quantitatively extracted. Despite the nominally frequency-pinned parametric operation, weak frequency pulling and OEPO mode softening were observed, revealing an additional adaptive interferometric degree of freedom introduced by the MZI architecture. By comparing local and global sampling configurations, we demonstrate that the YIG branch behaves predominantly as a local dispersive resonant subsystem governed by the complex magnonic susceptibility, whereas the phase-shifter branch primarily controls the global interferometric redistribution geometry. Nevertheless, coherent recombination and adaptive regeneration within the loop produce finite cross-coupling between the two branches, resulting in partially synchronized interferometric dynamics and branch-dependent adaptive redistribution. Quantitative complex-Lorentzian analysis further reveals substantial phase-to-amplitude conversion and distinct differences between the OEO and OEPO regimes: the phase-pinned OEPO favors strongly dispersive local YIG response, while the frequency-adaptive OEO exhibits more mixed absorptive--dispersive behavior due to spectral relaxation through frequency pulling. Broadly, the present platform establishes a versatile framework for exploring adaptive nonlinear interferometric physics, coherent phase redistribution, and branch-dependent synchronization phenomena in hybrid magnonic-photonic oscillator systems.

</details>

<a id="paper-2606.07862"></a>
### [Crystallizing Substrates Drag Supported Nanoparticles](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07862v1)

**Authors:** Cheng-Yu Chen, Duncan Burns, Peter W. Voorhees, Eric A. Stach  
**Type:** both · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.07862v1>

**Summary.** This work demonstrates that crystallization fronts act as powerful, deterministic drivers for nanoparticle movement. By combining in situ TEM with phase-field simulations, the authors prove that the resulting asymmetric surface energy gradient pulls nanoparticles along with the advancing crystal interface. This establishes a general principle for understanding transport driven by structural phase boundaries.

**Why it may be interesting.** While focused on condensed matter physics, the concept of a deterministic transport mechanism driven by non-equilibrium thermodynamic gradients (like those found at moving interfaces) is highly relevant to open quantum systems where environmental coupling drives system evolution.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** To determine the mechanism by which a propagating surface-energy boundary, such as during crystallization, can deterministically drive the transport of supported nanoparticles.

**Main result.** Propagating crystallization fronts actively drag supported Pt nanoparticles over long distances due to asymmetric particle-substrate interfacial energy environments. This process is governed by curvature gradients that redistribute mass and displace the particles.

**Method.** The study combines in situ TEM tracking of nanoparticle motion with time-resolved diffraction/4D-STEM analysis of support crystallization, supplemented by phase-field simulations.

**Model / system.** The system involves Pt nanoparticles supported on amorphous $	ext{AlO}_x$ thin films undergoing an amorphous-to-crystalline transformation into $	ext{Al}_2	ext{O}_3$. The theoretical framework models the driving force using surface energy gradients and curvature effects.

**Key observables.** Particle number evolution over time, SAED patterns confirming crystal phases ($\gamma$-, $	heta$-, $\alpha$-$	ext{Al}_2	ext{O}_3$), particle-free regions visualized by 4D-STEM virtual crystallinity maps.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Temperature ($700^\circ	ext{C}$ to $800^\circ	ext{C}$), crystallization front velocity, and the magnitude of surface energy contrast ($\Delta \gamma$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The model assumes that particle motion is driven solely by the interfacial energy contrast created by the moving front; it also relies on assumptions about equilibrium contact angles in simulations.

**Figures summary.** Figures show temperature-dependent particle evolution (Fig. 1), correlating rapid migration with the onset of crystalline alumina reflections via SAED, and simulation snapshots illustrating mass flow driven by curvature gradients near the advancing front (Fig. 4).

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from initial observations of particle immobilization upon crystallization to detailed in situ tracking experiments confirming temporal correlation between front advance and migration, culminating in phase-field simulations that mathematically prove the mechanism via surface energy gradient analysis.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

When a solid support undergoes crystallization, the advancing amorphous-to-crystalline transformation front separates regions of distinct surface energy, creating a moving interfacial energy boundary. A supported nanoparticle straddling such a boundary experiences an asymmetric particle-substrate interfacial energy environment that constitutes a lateral thermodynamic driving force for migration. Here, using in situ transmission electron microscopy to track Pt nanoparticle motion statistically, paired with time-resolved diffraction and 4D-STEM analysis to characterize support crystallization, we demonstrate that propagating crystallization fronts in amorphous AlO$_x$ thin films actively drag supported Pt nanoparticles over long distances. Temporal correlation between the onsets of support crystallization and rapid particle migration, together with 4D-STEM virtual crystallinity maps, establishes that the front drives particle motion. Phase-field simulations confirm that particle-substrate interfacial energy contrast alone sustains particle drag, and identify curvature gradients along the particle surface as the mechanism by which the advancing front redistributes mass and displaces the particle. These results establish a general mechanism by which any propagating surface-energy boundary on a substrate can act as a deterministic driver of supported nanoparticle transport.

</details>

### [Correlation enhanced resistance hysteresis near half filling in MoS2/WSe2 heterobilayer](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09244v1)

**Authors:** Yong Chen, Weikang Zhang, Meizhen Huang, Shengling Xiang, Zishu Zhou, Yaqi Ma, Chenxuan Lou, Haoxi Ji, Aoqian Zhang, Yifei Jin, Liheng An, Zefei Wu, Chun Cheng, Ning Wang
